OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 46-year-old male patient, with a known history of
 
 human immunodeficiency virus
 
 and
 
 hepatitis C virus
 
 coinfection, underwent a computed tomography (CT) to assess liver lesions detected by US, which were later identified as haemangiomas. On physical examination, hypoplastic fingernails were found.
 Imaging Findings: Incidentally, the CT revealed the presence of bilateral osseous processes projecting from the posterior aspect of the iliac bones (Figures 1a and 1b). Anteroposterior and lateral knee radiographs showed bilateral patellar hypoplasia associated with luxation (Figure 2a), while elbow radiographs demonstrated bilateral radial head hypoplasia with radio-humeral dislocation and abnormal radial curvature (Figure 2b).

model_answer_raw:
<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>
A) Key findings:
- Patient is a 46-year-old male with HIV and hepatitis C coinfection.
- Incidentally found bilateral osseous processes projecting from the posterior ilia.
- Bilateral nail hypoplasia.
- Bilateral patellar hypoplasia with luxation.
- Bilateral radial head hypoplasia with radiohumeral dislocation and abnormal radial curvature.
- Hemangiomas in the liver, which may or may not be related to the skeletal findings.

B) Candidate pass/fail:
1) Nail–patella syndrome:
   Pros: Classic features include nail dysplasia (hypoplastic nails), absent or hypoplastic patellae, and iliac horns (posterior osseous processes on the ilia). Elbow anomalies may include radial head dislocation and abnormal radial curvature. This matches the imaging findings well.
   Cons: None significant; findings are textbook for this syndrome.

2) Coffin–Siris syndrome:
   Pros: Can have nail anomalies.
   Cons: Predominant features are fifth finger and toe nail hypoplasia and aplasia with fifth finger hypoplasia, developmental delay, and distinct facial features. Skeletal findings do not typically include iliac horns, patellar hypoplasia, or radial head dislocation.

3) Meier–Gorlin syndrome:
   Pros: Includes patellar hypoplasia possible.
   Cons: Classic features include microtia, growth retardation, and patellar hypoplasia but not nail dysplasia or iliac horns. Radial head anomalies are not typical.

4) Coxopodopatellar syndrome:
   Pr
   Pros: Features include patellar agenesis or hypoplasia, elbow anomalies.
   Cons: Nail defects are not a hallmark; iliac horns not described. Also, this syndrome generally presents with facial dysmorphisms and intellectual disability.

5) Genitopatellar syndrome:
   Pros: Patellar agenesis/hypoplasia is a known feature.
   Cons: Nail hypoplasia and iliac horns are not typical. Main features include genital anomalies, renal anomalies, and severe psychomotor delay.

C) Ranking:
1) Nail–patella syndrome - best match due to nail hypoplasia, bilateral iliac horns (posterior osseous processes), patellar hypoplasia with luxation, and bilateral radial head hypoplasia with dislocation.
2) Coxopodopatellar syndrome - some overlapping features (patellar hypoplasia, elbow anomalies), but lacks nail changes and iliac horns.
3) Genitopatellar syndrome - patellar anomalies but lacks nail and hip findings.
4) Meier–Gorin syndrome - patellar hypoplasia possible but lacks nail and elbow/iliac features.
5) Coffin–Siris syndrome - nail hypoplasia present but no iliac or patellar abnormalities.

<final> Nailâpatella syndrome </final><|return|>
options: Nail–patella syndrome | Coffin–Siris syndrome | Meier–Gorlin syndrome | Coxopodopatellar syndrome | Genitopatellar syndrome

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: An 81-year-old male with a history of paroxysmal atrial fibrillation presents to the emergency department with a one-week onset of orthopnoea and exertional dyspnoea. Laboratory tests demonstrate elevated troponins, the electrocardiogram reveals a third-degree atrioventricular block, and the echocardiogram shows right ventricular thickening and pericardial effusion.
 Imaging Findings: A PET-CT scan (Figure 1) was carried out revealing a large hypermetabolic myocardial tissue. It extended through the right chambers and around the large vessels, sparing the left ventricle. Additionally, a hypermetabolic nodular image was identified at the left lung base.
 
 Therefore, a contrast-enhanced chest CT scan was performed (Figures 2a, 2b, 2c and 2d). It confirmed the presence of an infiltrative hypodense mass in the myocardium. It involved the right atrium, right ventricle, interatrial septum, and atrioventricular groove. The mass caused mural thickening of the right cavities. A cuff encircled the right coronary artery along its course. A moderate pericardial effusion was also observed. Finally, the CT scan confirmed the solid nodule in the posterior segment of the left lower lobe.
 
 A CT-guided percutaneous biopsy of the lung nodule was made for histological examination.
PostDescription:

The mass caused mural thickening of the right cavities. A cuff encircled the right coronary artery along its course. A moderate pericardial effusion was also observed. Finally, the CT scan confirmed the solid nodule in the posterior segment of the left lower lobe. A CT-guided percutaneous biopsy of the lung nodule was made for histological examination.
DifferentialDiagnosisList: Angiosarcoma, Ig-G4 related disease, Primary cardiac lymphoma, Erdheim–Chester disease, Undifferentiated pleomorphic sarcoma
model_answer_raw:
<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
- 81-year-old male with paroxysmal atrial fibrillation presenting with orthopnoea, exertional dyspnoea, and third-degree AV block.  
- Elevated troponins and echocardiogram showing right ventricular thickening and pericardial effusion.  
- PET-CT reveals a large hypermetabolic myocardial tissue mass extending through the right chambers, interatrial septum, and around large vessels, sparing the left ventricle.  
- CT shows an infiltrative hypodense myocardial mass in the right atrium, right ventricle, interatrial septum, and atrioventricular groove with mural thickening, pericardial effusion, and encasement of the right coronary artery.  
- Hypermetabolic nodular lesion in the left lung base confirmed as solid nodule on CT, biopsied for histology.  

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
1) Angiosarcoma  
   - Pros: Frequently arise in the right atrium (most common site for primary cardiac angiosarcomas), infiltrative, hypermetabolic on PET, often cause pericardial effusion.  
   - Cons: Angiosarcoma tends to be more localized to right atrium; myocardial infiltration can be extramyocardial; no mention of hemorrhagic nature; right coronary artery encasement is less typical.  

2) Ig-G4 related disease  
   - Pros: Causes thickening of cardiac structures, can affect coronary arteries, and can cause pericardial effusion; sometimes shows hypermetabolic lesions due to inflammation.  
   - Cons: Rarely presents as a large discrete myocardial mass; usually shows diffuse thickening rather than an infiltrative mass; often affects other organ systems and would have systemic signs; nodular lung mass less typical.  

3) Primary cardiac lymphoma  
   - Pros: Usually presents as a large infiltrative mass involving the atria and myocardium; hypermetabolic on PET; may involve right chambers and cause conduction abnormalities (e.g., AV block); may be associated with nodal or extranodal lesions (like lung nodules); more common in elderly patients.  
   - Cons: Less commonly arises in right atrium than angiosarcoma, but can involve multiple structures as seen here.  

4) Erdheim–Chester disease  
   - Pros: Rare non-Langerhans cell histiocytosis; can affect the myocardium, especially in pericardium and right atrium; can cause pericardial effusion and coronary artery involvement (encasement); can have lung nodules.  
   - Cons: More commonly causes a spiculated or striated pattern of infiltration rather than a large infiltrative mass; hypermetabolism less typical; usually involves bone and other systems concurrently; rarer in this age group without other systemic signs.  

5) Undifferentiated pleomorphic sarcoma  
   - Pros: Can cause a large infiltrative cardiac mass; can be hypermetabolic; can involve multiple chambers.  
   - Cons: Generally affects left side of the heart; rare; does not usually encase coronary arteries; pericardial effusion can be present but less characteristic; concurrent pulmonary nodule less typical.  

C) Ranking:  
1) Primary cardiac lymphoma: fits well with large hypermetabolic myocardial infiltrative mass, right atrial and ventricular involvement, AV block, pericardial effusion, lung nodules, and coronary artery involvement. Age and presentation fit lymphoma more than other entities.  
2) Angiosarcoma: Right atrium involvement and hypermetabolism fits, but the widespread infiltration and coronary artery encasement are less typical.  
3) Erdheim–Chester disease: Coronary artery encasement and lung nodules support it, but infiltrative myocardial mass and AV block less typical; no bone disease mentioned.  
4) Ig-G4 related disease: Possible pericardial effusion and coronary involvement, but mass formation and lung nodule less typical.  
5) Undifferentiated pleomorphic sarcoma: Least likely due to right side involvement and clinical features.  

<final> Primary cardiac lymphoma </final><|return|>
options: Angiosarcoma | Ig-G4 related disease | Primary cardiac lymphoma | Erdheim–Chester disease | Undifferentiated pleomorphic sarcoma

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 37-year-old man presented to our department of surgery with complains of high-grade fever, chills, vomiting and abdominal pain in epigastric region for the last 7 days. Laboratory investigations revealed a raised total leukocyte count (TLC) (of 18,500 cells/μL) with increased absolute neutrophil count (80%), and mildly deranged alkaline phosphate levels (200 IU/L).
 Imaging Findings: Initial abdominal ultrasonography (USG) revealed hepatomegaly with a large left lobe liver abscess (350 cc) and signs of impending rupture. Mild prominence of intrahepatic bile ducts (Figures 1a and 1b) and a few intrahepatic veno-venous collaterals (measuring ~2 mm in diameter) were also noted between the branch of the middle and right hepatic vein (Figures 2a, 2b and 6).
 
 Doppler study showed echogenic content with absent colour flow at the porto-splenic confluence, suggesting thrombosis, along with collateral vessel formation at the porta hepatis, likely indicative of cavernous transformation (Figures 3a, 3b, 3c, 3d, 4a and 4b).
 
 Contrast-enhanced computed tomography (CECT) scan of the abdomen confirmed the abscess and thrombosis, additionally revealing pinpoint narrowing of the inferior vena cava (IVC) with a small segment thrombus (Figures 5a, 5b, 5c, 5d and 5e). Notably, pancreatic parenchymal varices were observed (involving the head, and body of the pancreas) draining directly into the splenic vein (Figures 3e and 4c).
 
 Magnetic resonance cholangiopancreatography (MRCP) was also performed (Figures 7 and 8), revealing prominent intrahepatic bile ducts in the left lobe of the liver and non-visualisation of the biliary channels distal to the common hepatic duct (CHD) consistent with biliary obstruction.

Magnetic resonance cholangiopancreatography (MRCP) was also performed, revealing prominent intrahepatic bile ducts in the left lobe of the liver and non-visualisation of the biliary channels distal to the common hepatic duct (CHD) consistent with biliary obstruction.

FinalDiagnosis: Pylephlebitis and pancreatic parenchymal varices secondary to hepatic abscess
model_answer_raw:
<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
- 37-year-old man with high-grade fever, chills, vomiting, epigastric pain for 7 days.  
- Laboratory: leukocytosis (TLC 18,500, neutrophils 80%) and mildly elevated alkaline phosphatase.  
- Imaging: large left lobe liver abscess (350 cc) with signs of impending rupture.  
- Hepatomegaly.  
- Portal venous thrombosis at porto-splenic confluence with cavernous transformation (collaterals at porta hepatis).  
- Intrahepatic bile ducts dilation in left lobe, with non-visualisation distal to CHD on MRCP (biliary obstruction).  
- Presence of intrahepatic veno-venous collaterals (between middle and right hepatic vein branches).  
- IVC narrow segment with thrombus.  
- Pancreatic parenchymal varices involving head and body of pancreas draining into splenic vein.  

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
1) Liver cirrhosis with portal hypertension and varices formation  
   - Pros: presence of portal cavernous transformation and varices.  
   - Cons: No evidence of chronic liver disease or cirrhosis described (hepatomegaly, not atrophy; absence of signs like nodular surface or typical cirrhotic changes), and the acute presentation with liver abscess and thrombosis is not typical for cirrhosis-induced varices.  
   - Verdict: Does not fully explain liver abscess or acute portal vein thrombosis in this context; less likely.  

2) Arteriovenous malformation (AVM)  
   - Pros: vascular abnormality.  
   - Cons: no direct evidence of arteriovenous shunt or early venous enhancement; findings are more consistent with thrombosis and collaterals from chronic thrombus rather than AVM.  
   - Verdict: Ruled out.  

3) Pylephlebitis and pancreatic parenchymal varices secondary to hepatic abscess  
   - Pros: All features align: liver abscess with associated portal vein thrombosis (pylephlebitis), leading to cavernous transformation; portal hypertension causing pancreatic parenchymal varices and biliary obstruction due to external compression or venous congestion; consistent with acute septic thrombophlebitis.  
   - Cons: None significant; findings support this diagnosis well.  
   - Verdict: Strong candidate.  

4) Cystic lesions of pancreas  
   - Pros: pancreatic involvement.  
   - Cons: no cystic lesions described; pancreatic varices are vascular, not cystic; primary liver abscess with portal thrombosis not explained.  
   - Verdict: Ruled out.  

5) Intraductal papillary mucinous neoplasms (IPMNs)  
   - Pros: pancreatic ductal involvement possible.  
   - Cons: no mention of pancreatic ductal dilatation, cystic lesions, or mucinous content; primary pathology is liver abscess and portal thrombosis; pancreatic findings are varices, not neoplasm.  
   - Verdict: Ruled out.  

C) Ranking:  
1) Pylephlebitis and pancreatic parenchymal varices secondary to hepatic abscess (strongly supported by findings of liver abscess, portal vein thrombosis, cavernous transformation, pancreatic varices and biliary obstruction).  
2) Liver cirrhosis with portal hypertension and varices formation (does not explain acute abscess and thrombosis well).  
3) Arteriovenous malformation (no imaging support).  
4) Intraductal papillary mucinous neoplasms (unsupported).  
5) Cystic lesions of pancreas (unsupported).  

<final>  
Pylephlebitis and pancreatic parenchymal varices secondary to hepatic abscess  
</final><|return|>
model_answer: Pylephlebitis and pancreatic parenchymal varices secondary to hepatic abscess
options: Liver cirrhosis with portal hypertension and varices formation | Arteriovenous malformation (AVM) | Pylephlebitis and pancreatic parenchymal varices secondary to hepatic abscess | Cystic lesions of pancreas | Intraductal papillary mucinous neoplasms

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 31-year-old male presented at the urologist with scrotal swelling for 4 weeks. He had no relevant history. Clinically there was a voluminous solid swelling of the right hemiscrotum and retraction of the penis. Blood results showed extremely high alpha-fetoprotein levels >53.000 µg/L.
 Imaging Findings: Ultrasound showed a large heterogeneous mass in the right hemiscrotum with solid and cystic/necrotic components and vascularisation (Figures 1, 2 and 3). There was no visualisation of normal testicular parenchyma or epididymis on the right. There was a normal left testis and epididymis (not shown).
 
 CT showed, besides the large heterogeneous mass in the right hemiscrotum (Figure 4), no metastasis or lymphadenopathies.
PostDescription:
Clinical History: A 31-year-old male presented at the urologist with scrotal swelling for 4 weeks. He had no relevant history. Clinically there was a voluminous solid swelling of the right hemiscrotum and retraction of the penis. Blood results showed extremely high alpha-fetoprotein levels >53.000 µg/L.
 Imaging Findings: Ultrasound showed a large heterogeneous mass in the right hemiscrotum with solid and cystic/necrotic components and vascularisation. There was no visualisation of normal testicular parenchyma or epididymis on the right. There was a normal left testis and epididymis (not shown). CT showed, besides the large heterogeneous mass in the right hemiscrotum, no metastasis or lymphadenopathies.
DifferentialDiagnosisList: Mixed germinal cell tumour, Pure yolk sac tumour, Seminoma, Choriocarcinoma, Teratoma, Lymphoma
model_answer_raw:
<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>
A) Key findings:
- 31-year-old male with a voluminous solid swelling in the right hemiscrotum and penis retraction.
- Extremely high alpha-fetoprotein (AFP) levels >53,000 µg/L.
- Ultrasound shows a large heterogeneous mass with solid and cystic/necrotic components and vascularisation.
- No visualisation of normal testicular parenchyma or epididymis on the right side.
- Normal left testis and epididymis.
- CT confirms the right hemiscrotal mass with no metastasis or lymphadenopathy.

B) Candidate pass/fail with pros/cons:

1) Mixed germinal cell tumour:
   - Pros: Can occur at this age; often presents as a solid mass with solid and cystic/necrotic areas.
   - Cons: AFP elevation is variable; does not specifically explain extremely high AFP levels without a specific component.

2) Pure yolk sac tumour:
   - Pros: Yolk sac tumours are known to produce extremely high AFP levels (often >100,000 µg/L); typically occur in younger males; usually solid with cystic/necrotic components.
   - Cons: Rare in adults but possible; the presentation fits well with the AFP level and imaging features.

3) Seminoma:
   - Pros: Common testicular tumour in young men.
   - Cons: Does not produce elevated AFP; AFP of this magnitude rules out seminoma.

4) Choriocarcinoma:
   - Pros: Aggressive testicular tumour.
   - Cons: Typically associated with elevated hCG, not AFP; AFP levels are not markedly elevated in choriocarcinoma.

5) Teratoma:
   - Pros: Can show heterogeneous appearance with solid and cystic components.
   - Cons: AFP elevation is usually not extremely high or absent; teratomas alone rarely produce such high AFP.

6) Lymphoma:
   - Pros: Can present as testicular mass in this age group.
   - Cons: Typically does not elevate AFP significantly; AFP >53,000 µg/L rules out lymphoma.

C) Ranking:
1) Pure yolk sac tumour - best fits extremely high AFP and imaging findings.
2) Mixed germinal cell tumour - possible but less specific AFP level.
3) Teratoma - imaging compatible but AFP level less typical.
4) Lymphoma - AFP level not compatible.
5) Choriocarcinoma - AFP not characteristic.
6) Seminoma - AFP not compatible.

<final>Pure yolk sac tumour</final><|return|>
options: Mixed germinal cell tumour | Pure yolk sac tumour | Seminoma | Choriocarcinoma | Teratoma | Lymphoma

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 39-year-old male presented with sudden-onset left-sided weakness and mild slurring of speech lasting two days, accompanied by forgetfulness, particularly difficulty in retaining new information. Neurological examination showed left
 
 hemiparesis in the left upper and lower extremities, with intact cranial nerve functions.
 Imaging Findings: MRI
 
 revealed acute watershed infarcts in the right centrum semiovale (Figures 1a, 1b, 1c and 1d). Sulcal hyperintensity in the right temporal region (ivy sign) was noted (Figure 1d).
 
 CT demonstrated a hypoplastic carotid canal on the left (Figure 2).
 
 CT angiography (CTA) showed left internal carotid artery hypoplasia and focal stenosis of bilateral supraclinoid
 
 internal carotid arteries (
 
 ICAs) (Figure 3). The left
 
 middle
 
 cerebral artery (
 
 MCA) was supplied via the posterior circulation, while the left
 
 anterior cerebral artery (
 
 ACA) was perfused through the
 
 anterior communicating (
 
 ACom) artery. The foetal origin of the left
 
 posterior cerebral artery (
 
 PCA) was identified.
 
 Digital subtraction angiography (
 
 DSA) confirmed these findings, showing no “puff of smoke” appearance (Figures 4a and 4b) and indicating an absence of collateral formation.
PostDescription:
Clinical History: A 39-year-old male presented with sudden-onset left-sided weakness and mild slurring of speech lasting two days, accompanied by forgetfulness, particularly difficulty in retaining new information. Neurological examination showed left hemiparesis in the left upper and lower extremities, with intact cranial nerve functions.
 Imaging Findings: MRI revealed acute watershed infarcts in the right centrum semiovale. Sulcal hyperintensity in the right temporal region (ivy sign) was noted. CT demonstrated a hypoplastic carotid canal on the left. CT angiography (CTA) showed left internal carotid artery hypoplasia and focal stenosis of bilateral supraclinoid internal carotid arteries (ICAs). The left middle cerebral artery (MCA) was supplied via the posterior circulation, while the left anterior cerebral artery (ACA) was perfused through the anterior communicating (ACom) artery. The foetal origin of the left posterior cerebral artery (PCA) was identified. Digital subtraction angiography (DSA) confirmed these findings, showing no “puff of smoke” appearance and indicating an absence of collateral formation.
DifferentialDiagnosisList: Atherosclerotic ICA stenosis with secondary ischemic events, Primary Moyamoya syndrome with bilateral involvement, Moyamoya disease with ICA hypoplasia, ICA dissection causing ischemia, Vasculitis-induced stenosis of cerebral arteries
model_answer_raw:
<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
- 39-year-old male with acute left-sided hemiparesis and mild speech slurring, with recent forgetfulness.  
- MRI: acute watershed infarcts in right centrum semiovale. Sulcal hyperintensity in right temporal region ("ivy sign") suggesting slow or collateral flow.  
- CT: hypoplastic left carotid canal.  
- CTA and DSA: left ICA hypoplasia + focal stenosis of bilateral supraclinoid ICAs. Left MCA supply from posterior circulation via posterior communicating artery and left ACA perfusion via anterior communicating artery. Left PCA has fetal origin. No moyamoya collaterals ("puff of smoke") observed.  
- No evidence of carotid dissection or vasculitis.  

B) Candidate pass/fail with 1-2 pros/cons each:  
1) Atherosclerotic ICA stenosis with secondary ischemic events  
- Pros: Focal stenosis of bilateral supraclinoid ICAs could be atherosclerotic.  
- Cons: Patient is relatively young for atherosclerosis; presence of hypoplastic left carotid canal and carotid artery hypoplasia is not typical for atherosclerosis; ivy sign and watershed infarcts are more characteristic of moyamoya pattern.  

2) Primary Moyamoya syndrome with bilateral involvement  
- Pros: Bilateral ICA stenosis and ischemic events are typical features. Ivy sign is often seen in moyamoya.  
- Cons: Absence of moyamoya collaterals ("puff of smoke") goes against classic moyamoya disease; also, presence of ICA hypoplasia with hypoplastic carotid canal is not typical for classic Moyamoya (which is a progressive steno-occlusive process without developmental hypoplasia).  

3) Moyamoya disease with ICA hypoplasia  
- Pros: Bilateral ICA stenosis plus abnormal vascular supply patterns, ivy sign, and ICA hypoplasia with hypoplastic carotid canal (developmental anomaly) fits moyamoya disease features, especially with hypoplastic ICA as a variant. Bilateral involvement with collateral recruitment from posterior circulation and ACom artery is characteristic.  
- Cons: Absence of moyamoya collaterals is somewhat atypical, but this variant may show less prominent collaterals early in disease or due to congenital hypoplasia rather than progressive stenosis.  

4) ICA dissection causing ischemia  
- Pros: ICA stenosis can cause ischemia.  
- Cons: No imaging evidence of dissection flap or intramural hematoma; bilateral stenosis and hypoplastic carotid canal argue against dissection; onset gradual rather than classic dissection presentation.  

5) Vasculitis-induced stenosis of cerebral arteries  
- Pros: Can cause bilateral ICA stenosis.  
- Cons: No clinical or imaging signs of vasculitis (e.g., vessel wall enhancement, inflammatory signs); hypoplastic carotid canal suggests congenital/structural anomaly; vasculitis unlikely in a young patient without systemic signs.  

C) Ranking:  
1) Moyamoya disease with ICA hypoplasia  
2) Primary Moyamoya syndrome with bilateral involvement  
3) Atherosclerotic ICA stenosis with secondary ischemic events  
4) Vasculitis-induced stenosis of cerebral arteries  
5) ICA dissection causing ischemia  

<final>  
Moyamoya disease with ICA hypoplasia  
</final><|return|>
options: Atherosclerotic ICA stenosis with secondary ischemic events | Primary Moyamoya syndrome with bilateral involvement | Moyamoya disease with ICA hypoplasia | ICA dissection causing ischemia | Vasculitis-induced stenosis of cerebral arteries

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 55-year-old female presented with status epilepticus and focal seizures in the left arm, with progressive behavioural disturbance, and altered sensorium over 4 weeks duration.
 
 The patient is
 
 HBsAg
 
 positive; otherwise, all laboratory findings were within the normal limits. Neurological and other systemic examinations were within the normal limits and no cranial nerve palsy was detected.
 Imaging Findings: Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) brain with contrast was done to find the aetiology behind it, and it revealed a lesion in the supratentorial compartment, centred within the deep white matter in the right frontal cortex.
 
 A cerebrospinal
 
 fluid (C
 
 SF) culture was done, and it showed an oligoclonal band suggestive of intrathecal IgG antibody synthesis. Patient was started on salmeterol and clinicians finds significant improvement in seizures.

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
1) Tumefactive demyelination with open ring sign  
   - Pros: Tumefactive demyelination often presents with mass effect in deep white matter, subacute presentation, seizures, behavioral changes, and CSF oligoclonal bands. The lesion location fits.  
   - Cons: The explicit mention of "open ring sign" is missing; no direct mention of radiologic sign of open ring on MRI.

   - Pros: Similar to above; subacute onset, seizures, frontal lobar white matter lesion, CSF oligoclonal bands support demyelination.  
   - Cons: No specific mention of ring enhancement or other classic features, but clinical presentation fits well.  
3) CNS neoplasm  
   - Pros: Lesion in deep white matter could be neoplasm; seizures and progressive symptoms could fit.  
   - Cons: No mention of mass effect, nodular enhancement, or other neoplastic features; CSF oligoclonal bands less typical.  
4) Brain metastasis  
   - Pros: Could present as deep white matter lesion with seizures.  
   - Cons: No known primary cancer; HBsAg positivity not a known cancer risk; CSF oligoclonal bands not typical; metastatic lesions usually have rim enhancement and edema.  
5) Brain abscess  
   - Pros: Can present with focal lesions, seizures.  
   - Cons: Usually shows ring-enhancing lesion with central necrosis; CSF may show infection; patient no fever or systemic infection signs; CSF oligoclonal bands not typical.  
6) Granulomatous disease  
   - Pros: Possible in HBsAg positive patients depending on context.  
   - Cons: No granulomatous lesions or signs described; CSF oligoclonal bands not typical.  
7) Vasculitis  
   - Pros: Can cause seizures, behavioral changes, white matter lesions.  
   - Cons: No mention of vascular imaging findings; CSF oligoclonal bands less typical; lesion described as localized mass, not multifocal vascular strokes.  
8) Acute demyelinating encephalitis  
   - Pros: Similar to tumefactive demyelination; fits clinical picture and CSF findings; lesion location fits.  
   - Cons: Usually more diffuse or multifocal; tumefactive lesions are a subtype with mass effect.

C) Ranking:  
1) Tumefactive demyelinating lesion (fits clinical and radiologic features, CSF oligoclonal bands, deep white matter lesion, subacute presentation).  
2) Acute demyelinating encephalitis (similar but usually less mass effect).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 40-year-old man with lower abdominal pain received a CT abdomen/pelvis in the emergency department to rule out appendicitis. An incidental fat attenuation mass was present in the right ischiorectal fossa. Five years later, the patient presented again with pelvic pain and received a CT pelvis.
 Imaging Findings: The initial CT study identified a right ischiorectal fossa fat attenuation mass displacing the anal canal towards the left without enhancing soft tissue, fluid or calcifications (Figures 1a and 1b). There was a thin capsule surrounding the mass.
 
 When the patient presented again five years later, a CT pelvis showed that the mass had significantly enlarged (Figures 2a, 2b, and 2c). It contained two well-defined cystic compartments with homogeneous fluid attenuation (15 HU) and an irregular enhancing soft tissue rim in the posterosuperior compartment.
 
 An MR pelvis done shortly thereafter recapitulated these findings but revealed that the apparently homogeneous central collection on CT contained a mix of fluid and soft tissue, with both thin and thick septations (Figures 3a, 3b, 4a, and 4b). Additionally, the entire mass had a thick, irregular rim of enhancement.
PostDescription:

<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>
A) Key findings:
- 40-year-old man with lower abdominal and pelvic pain.
- Incidental fat attenuation mass in right ischiorectal fossa, displacing the anal canal to the left.
- Thin capsule, no enhancement, fluid, or calcifications on initial CT.
- Five years later: mass significantly enlarged, with two well-defined cystic compartments containing homogeneous fluid (15 HU) and an irregular enhancing soft tissue rim in one compartment.
- MRI shows mixed fluid and soft tissue in the central collection, with thin and thick septations.
- Thick, irregular rim of enhancement around the entire mass.

B) Candidate pass/fail:
1) Extragonadal germ cell tumour
   - Pros: Can occur in pelvis, may have cystic and solid components.
   - Cons: Rare in ischiorectal fossa; usually not fat attenuation; no mention of typical germ cell markers or patient demographics; less likely for this location.

2) Ischiorectal liposarcoma
   - Pros: Fat attenuation mass in ischiorectal fossa fits location; malignant lesions can enlarge and develop heterogeneous components and enhancement.
   - Cons: Liposarcoma often has thick septations, nodules; usually no well-defined cystic compartments; enhancement pattern may differ; initial thin capsule less typical.

3) Dermoid cyst
   - Pros: Contains fat and fluid; typical is mass in pelvis with fat attenuation; may have calcification (none here), well-defined cystic components.
   - Cons: Dermoid cysts usually have fat-fluid levels and sometimes calcifications; location in ischiorectal fossa less common; thick enhancing rim unusual.

4) Tailgut cyst
   - Pros: Retrorectal (presacral) cystic mass; can have multiloculated cysts with enhancing septa; usually located in midline or slightly off-midline presacral space.
   - Cons: Ischiorectal fossa is a different space (lateral to rectum and below levator ani), not the typical presacral location; not classically fat attenuation; location mismatch.

5) Perineal epidermal cyst
   - Pros: Can occur in perineal/gluteal region; cystic with possible enhancement of capsule.
   - Cons: Usually soft tissue density without fat attenuation; rare in ischiorectal fossa; fat attenuation mass less typical.

6) Anterior sacral meningocele
   - Pros: Retrorectal cystic mass; fluid attenuation.
   - Cons: Anterior sacral meningoceles are CSF-filled sacs communicating with the sacral subarachnoid space; not fat attenuation; no fat component; location is presacral, not ischiorectal fossa.

C) Ranking:
1) Ischiorectal liposarcoma - fits fat attenuation mass in ischiorectal fossa, enlarges over 5 years with enhancement and cystic changes; fat component with thick capsule; plausible.
2) Dermoid cyst - fat and fluid components, but no calcification; uncommon in ischiorectal fossa; cystic features with enhancement less typical.
3) Tailgut cyst - location less typical (presacral vs ischiorectal), no fat component.
4) Perineal epidermal cyst - no fat attenuation, not typical.
5) Extragonadal germ cell tumor - rare location and lacks typical features.
6) Anterior sacral meningocele - no fat attenuation, wrong location.

Given the location (right ischiorectal fossa), fat attenuation mass, enlargement with cystic components and enhancement, ischiorectal liposarcoma is most consistent, especially given the malignant potential for cystic degeneration and irregular enhancement.

<final>Ischiorectal liposarcoma</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 55-year-old female patient was admitted to our oncology centre with follicular lymphoma. She had a seven-kilogram weight loss over one year, loss of appetite, and fever of unknown origin. There is no significant medical history, and physical examination revealed only cervical lymphadenopathy.
 
 The patient’s blood workup revealed a slight increase in C-reactive protein (1.2 mg/dL) and creatine kinase (151 U/L), with no other abnormal laboratory findings.
 Imaging Findings: Computed tomography detected a large lesion, measuring 7.5 x 5 centimetres, heterogeneous, with fluid densities and lobulated margins in the superior segments of the right hepatic lobe (Figure 1). Due to the
 
 patient’s
 
 clinical context, an ultrasound-guided biopsy was performed, which revealed an unrepresentative fragment of liver tissue, encompassing a fibro-hyalinised nodule.
 
 An abdominal magnetic resonance (MR) was performed to characterise the hepatic lesion further (Figures 2, 3a, 3b, and 4). The MR revealed a heterogeneous, complex cystic lesion with a thin wall, hypointense on T2-weighted images, and hyperintense on T1-weighted images, located within the right liver lobe. The lesion exhibited radiating septa towards the periphery, appearing as low-signal intensity on T2-weighted images and high-signal intensity on T1-weighted images. These septa delineated multiple smaller cysts, hypointense on T1 and hyperintense on T2-weighted images, occupying almost the entire lesion. After contrast administration, the lesion did not exhibit restricted diffusion and no significant enhancement of the soft tissue component.

   - Pros: Complex cystic lesion with septa and cysts, location in right hepatic lobe consistent with hydatid cyst features. Radiating septa and internal cysts could represent daughter cysts typical of hydatid disease. Thin wall cyst with heterogenous content fits.
   - Cons: No mention of typical features such as calcifications, membranes, or "water lily sign," though these may not be mandatory. Clinical context (lymphoma) may be misleading.

2) Cystic/necrotic liver metastasis
   - Pros: Patient has lymphoma (follicular), which can metastasize to liver, and necrosis/cystic changes in metastases can occur. Heterogeneity fits.
   - Cons: No significant enhancement of soft tissue component, which is unusual for metastasis. Also, the presence of multiple small cysts with radiating septa is atypical for metastases. Typically, metastases enhance viable tissue or have rim enhancement.

3) Hepatic abscess
   - Pros: Fever, weight loss, elevated CRP may support abscess. Complex cystic lesion with septa can be seen in abscesses.
   - Cons: No restricted diffusion on MR (abscesses typically show restricted diffusion due to pus), lack of significant rim enhancement, and the lesion's morphology (radiating septa with multiple smaller cysts) is atypical for abscess. Also, biopsy showed fibro-hyalinised nodule, not inflammatory/infectious cells.

4) Biliary cystadenoma/cystadenocarcinoma
   - Pros: Cystic lesion with septations could suggest cystadenoma; these lesions often occur in middle-aged women.
   - Cons: Typically show enhancing septa/wall, sometimes solid components. The lack of enhancement and presence of multiple smaller cysts favour hydatid disease over cystadenoma/cystadenocarcinoma. Also, the described lesion size and complex internal architecture match hydatid more.

C) Ranking:
1) Hepatic hydatid disease (type IIb) - best fits lesion morphology with radiating septa and smaller cysts (daughter cysts), compatible with type IIb hydatid cysts (complex cystic with daughter cysts).
2) Biliary cystadenoma/cystadenocarcinoma - possible but lack of enhancement and typical solid components discounts favorably.
3) Cystic/necrotic liver metastasis - possible necrosis, but lack of enhancement and internal cysts is less likely.
4) Hepatic abscess - unlikely given lack of restricted diffusion and lack of typical abscess features.

Therefore, the lesion most likely represents hepatic hydatid disease (type IIb), despite no mention of classic signs; the radiating septa and multiple internal smaller cysts strongly point to this diagnosis.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A one-year-old full-term-born male child, born to non-consanguineous parents, presents with complaints of thoracic deformity. No history of previous surgeries.
 Imaging Findings: The AP chest radiograph (Figure 1) demonstrated the absence of ribs in the right mid-zone, with fusion of multiple adjacent ribs. Fusion of multiple ribs was also noted on the left side. The image showed evidence of dextroscoliosis.
 
 Further evaluation with computed tomography revealed bifid ribs with segmental fusion and widening at multiple levels bilaterally (Videos and Figures 2a, 2b, 2c, 3, 4, and 5).
 
 Fusion of posterior vertebral elements was observed at multiple levels, and dextroscoliosis was noted involving the thoracic spine. Posterior vertebral arch defects were identified involving the L5 vertebra and all sacral vertebrae (Figures 2b and 2c).
 
 Mild volume loss of the right hemithorax was noted. The pulmonary parenchyma, bronchial, and vascular markings appeared normal bilaterally.

- Pulmonary parenchyma, bronchial, and vascular markings appear normal bilaterally.

1) Spondylocostal dysostosis (Jarcho–Levin syndrome):  
   - Pros: Presence of rib anomalies (bifid ribs, segmental fusion, widening), vertebral segmentation defects.  
   - Cons: Typically associated with multiple levels of vertebral segmentation anomalies but preservation of rib alignment is more common; rib anomalies may be less severe compared to spondylothoracic dysostosis.  

2) Spondylothoracic dysostosis (Lavy–Moseley syndrome):  
   - Pros: Severe vertebral segmentation anomalies (posterior element fusion), rib anomalies with extensive fusion and bifid ribs.

C) Ranking:

2) Spondylocostal dysostosis (Jarcho–Levin syndrome) - While it also has vertebral and rib anomalies, the severity and pattern described more closely matches the LavyâMoseley syndrome.

Clinical History: A 17-month-old boy presented with a 3-day history of sternal swelling and pain. No fever or any
 
 other
 
 symptoms. There was no history of trauma. Physical examination revealed a 2 cm swelling in the manubriosternal region. Inflammatory parameters were normal except for
 
 a slightly elevated value for C-reactive protein.

No significant irregularity of the bony surfaces at the manubriosternal synchondrosis is visible.
 
 However, a separation between the manubrial and sternal ossification centres is evident
 
 , accompanied by densification of the adjacent subcutaneous tissue (Figures 2a and 2b).
 
 Ultrasound revealed a hypoechoic and heterogeneous, dumbbell-shaped lesion centred on the manubriosternal synchondrosis. This lesion crosses the synchondrosis, extending superficially into the subcutaneous tissue and deeply to the depth of the chest wall/anterior mediastinum. Colour Doppler ultrasound demonstrated no internal vascularisation. Additionally, there is a thickening of the soft tissues surrounding the manubriosternal joint (Figures 3a, 3b, and 3c].
 
 Bone scintigraphy showed no significant abnormalities.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A newborn with ambiguous genitalia and multiple congenital anomalies was admitted in March 2023. Born preterm at 34 weeks, the infant faced respiratory distress, electrolyte imbalances, and sepsis.
 Imaging Findings: The patient presented with extensive congenital anomalies, including an omphalocele with liver and bowel herniating through a membrane-covered defect, bladder exstrophy, and imperforate anus.
 
 Spinal imaging revealed fusion defects in the lower thoracic and lumbar vertebrae, with an associated cystic mass with dystrophic neural elements. Cranial imaging showed a low-lying torcular Herophili, ventriculomegaly, fullness of the foramen magnum, and possible aqueductal stenosis. Other imaging findings included ectopic kidneys (one partially thoracic and another pelvic), midgut malrotation, sacral agenesis, rib synostosis, pubic symphysis diastasis, ambiguous genitalia, and other musculoskeletal abnormalities, highlighting extensive multi-system involvement.
 
 Due to the patient’s medical instability and eventual demise, as well as lack of resources, further imaging could not assess other abnormalities including undescended testes and detailed evaluation of the urinary system. These structural abnormalities posed considerable diagnostic and management challenges.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: An 85-year-old woman with no significant past medical history presented with a rapidly growing mass on her left shoulder (Figure 1), with no history of trauma.
 
 On physical examination, the mass was hard and non-mobile.
 Imaging Findings: A shoulder X-ray and ultrasound scan were performed in order to rule out malignancy.
 
 The X-ray (Figure 2) shows a rounded, homogeneous soft-tissue density mass on the left shoulder, located just above the acromioclavicular joint (ACJ). Additionally, the humeral head is seen cranially displaced, with subsequent narrowing of the subacromial space. Osseous degenerative change can be seen in both the humeral head and the ACJ.
 
 On the US exam (Figures 3a, 3b, and 3c), the images show a supraclavicular fluid collection, predominantly anechogenic but heterogeneous, that appears to be erupting superiorly from the acromioclavicular (AC) interval. No Doppler signal is detected. On interrogation of the rotator cuff, the exam shows the absence of the supraspinatus and infraspinatus tendons over the humeral head, as well as bony contour irregularity.

On the US exam, the images show a supraclavicular fluid collection, predominantly anechogenic but heterogeneous, that appears to be erupting superiorly from the acromioclavicular (AC) interval. No Doppler signal is detected. On interrogation of the rotator cuff, the exam shows the absence of the supraspinatus and infraspinatus tendons over the humeral head, as well as bony contour irregularity.

 (Figure 2), compatible with an osteoma, and multiple well-delineated lesions of mature bone between the roots of the teeth compatible with hypercementosis.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A nine-year-old female child presented with fever, restriction of neck movements and posterior bulging of the mucosa of the posterior oropharyngeal/hypopharyngeal wall lateralised to the left side on nasopharyngolaryngoscopy. Piriform sinuses were inspected and did not show any irregularities.
 Imaging Findings: A contrast-enhanced CT scan of the neck revealed a well-defined oval fluid collection, with rim enhancement, in the retropharyngeal space, measuring 2 x 2 x 1 cm
 
 (ap/axial/cc) (
 
 rrows in Figures 1
 
 and
 
 2). Retropharyngeal space oedema was also present, from the skull base to the hypopharynx level (
 
 small arrow in Figure 2
 
 ). Additionally, a thin well-defined rim-
 
 enhancing
 
 fistulous tract could be identified, running from the inferior left border of the retropharyngeal collection to the upper pole of the left thyroid lobe (arrows on Figure 3).
PostDescription:
Clinical History: A nine-year-old female child presented with fever, restriction of neck movements and posterior bulging of the mucosa of the posterior oropharyngeal/hypopharyngeal wall lateralised to the left side on nasopharyngolaryngoscopy. Piriform sinuses were inspected and did not show any irregularities.
 Imaging Findings: A contrast-enhanced CT scan of the neck revealed a well-defined oval fluid collection, with rim enhancement, in the retropharyngeal space, measuring 2 x 2 x 1 cm. Retropharyngeal space oedema was also present, from the skull base to the hypopharynx level. Additionally, a thin well-defined rim-enhancing fistulous tract could be identified, running from the inferior left border of the retropharyngeal collection to the upper pole of the left thyroid lobe.
DifferentialDiagnosisList: Retropharyngeal abscess, Atypically located third or fourth infected branchial cleft cyst, Necrotic retropharyngeal adenopathy, Suppurative thyroiditis
FinalDiagnosis: Atypically located third or fourth infected branchial cleft cyst

- 9-year-old female with fever and restricted neck movements.

- Contrast-enhanced CT neck: well-defined oval fluid collection with rim enhancement in the retropharyngeal space (2 x 2 x 1 cm).  
- Retropharyngeal space oedema from skull base to hypopharynx.  
- Thin well-defined rim-enhancing fistulous tract from inferior left border of retropharyngeal collection to the upper pole of the left thyroid lobe.  

B) Candidate pass/fail with 1-2 pros/cons:  

1) Retropharyngeal abscess:  
- Pros: Fluid collection with rim enhancement in the retropharyngeal space; clinical presentation (fever, neck rigidity), compatible with retropharyngeal abscess.  
- Cons: Presence of a fistulous tract to the thyroid gland is not typical for a simple retropharyngeal abscess.  

2) Atypically located third or fourth infected branchial cleft cyst:  
- Pros: The lesion is lateralized to the left side, cystic, with rim enhancement; typical of infected branchial cleft cysts.  
- Cons: Location is retropharyngeal and deep, while branchial cleft cysts usually present more superficially in the neck (lateral neck) and rarely have a fistulous tract to the thyroid gland. The fistulous tract to the thyroid is uncommon for branchial cleft cysts.  

3) Necrotic retropharyngeal adenopathy:  
- Pros: Could present as a rim-enhancing fluid collection in the retropharyngeal space with edema; possible with necrotic lymph nodes.  
- Cons: No mention of necrotic lymph nodes, no typical lymph node morphology. Fistulous tract to thyroid is not typical for necrotic lymph nodes.  

4) Suppurative thyroiditis:  
- Pros: Fistulous tract connecting retropharyngeal collection to upper pole of left thyroid lobe suggests infection spreading to the thyroid; fever and neck restriction are compatible.  
- Cons: Primary retropharyngeal abscess is present; however, the fistulous tract suggests the primary infection may be related to the thyroid gland extending upward. Typically, supprative thyroiditis occurs in children with pyriform sinus fistula leading to secondary infection. The fistula origin to the thyroid pole is characteristic.  

C) Ranking with tie-breaks:  
- The presence of a fistulous tract connecting the retropharyngeal collection to the thyroid lobe is hallmark for suppurative thyroiditis due to a pyriform sinus fistula, which commonly presents as a left-sided neck abscess with a fistula to the thyroid.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: We present the case of a 62-year-old man with a distal oesophageal adenocarcinoma treated with neoadjuvant chemoradiotherapy who developed rapid swelling in the left preauricular region within hours after Ivor Lewis oesophagectomy. CT was performed to rule out active bleeding and postoperative haematoma.
 Imaging Findings: CT showed diffuse enlargement of the left parotid gland with increased heterogeneous uptake of contrast material, fat stranding in the left parotid space, and thickening of the adjacent superficial layer of the deep cervical fascia (Figures 1 and 3). Oedema and fat stranding extended slightly into the soft tissues of the ipsilateral buccal and masticator spaces, and the sternocleidomastoid muscle was enlarged (Figures 2 and 4). There was no involvement of deeper compartments.
 
 No haematomas or signs of active bleeding were observed.
PostDescription:

There was no involvement of deeper compartments. No haematomas or signs of active bleeding were observed.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 2-day-old male presented with non-bilious emesis and a left thigh cutaneous lesion. The patient was found to have pyloric atresia requiring gastroduodenostomy; this procedure led to intestinal malrotation, and the appendix was removed prophylactically. During surgery, the cutaneous lesion was also biopsied. The patient was transiently jaundiced following surgery; this resolved spontaneously, but a liver ultrasound was requested as part of the workup of this.
 Imaging Findings: Liver US showed a normal biliary tree and 2 hypoechoic liver lesions: the larger a 6 mm lesion in segment IVb (lesion 1). Contrast-enhanced ultrasound (CEUS) was performed to further characterise. Three lesions were examined with 4 boluses of ultrasound (Total vol. 1.1mL, Optison, GE Healthcare Inc., using a Phillips EPIQ machine). Lesion 1 had increased in size (seg. IVb, now 32 mm) (Figures 1a, 1b, 1c, and 1d); Lesions 2 (seg. II, 7 mm) (Figures 2a, 2b, 2c, and 2d) and 3 were also seen (seg. I,10 mm) (Figures 3a, 3b, 3c, and 3d).
 
 To further characterise, a liver MRI with hepatobiliary contrast was performed. Lesion 1 (34 mm) was T1 hypointense, T2 hyperintense, infiltrative, rectangular area with capsular retraction (Figures 4a, 4b and 4c). Lesions 2 (Figure 4d) and 3 were T1 hypointense with central T2 hyperintensity relative to the liver. No lesions showed enhancement, macroscopic fat, or diffusion restriction.
PostDescription:

model_answer_raw:
<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
- 2-day-old male infant with pyloric atresia and surgical history.  
- Non-bilious emesis, left thigh cutaneous lesion (biopsied).  
- Transient jaundice, normal biliary tree on US.  
- Liver lesions: initially 2 hypoechoic lesions (largest 6 mm seg IVb) on US; follow-up CEUS and liver MRI due to lesion growth and new lesions.  
- Lesion 1 grew from 6 mm to 34 mm over a few days, infiltrative, capsular retraction, T1 hypointense, T2 hyperintense, no enhancement, no fat, no diffusion restriction.  
- Lesions 2 and 3 also present, similar signal characteristics, no enhancement.  

B) Candidate pass/fail with 1-2 pros/cons each:  
1) Hepatoblastoma:  
   Pros: Common malignant liver tumor in infants; typically presents as a solid mass in the liver.  
   Cons: Usually shows enhancement with solid component; this lesion shows no enhancement. T1 hypointense and T2 hyperintense but no diffusion restriction or enhancement. Tumor is usually larger at presentation, not rapid size change in days. Also no mention of AFP levels. Lesions in hepatoblastoma can have calcifications but not mentioned.  

2) Lymphoma:  
   Pros: Can present as infiltrative liver lesions.  
   Cons: Rare in newborns; usually T2 hyperintense but would expect some enhancement. No diffusion restriction unusual for lymphoma. Typical systemic symptoms absent.  

3) Infantile myofibromatosis:  
   Pros: Occurs in infants; can cause cutaneous lesions (thigh lesion biopsied). Known to cause hepatic lesions that can be hypoechoic on US and T1 hypointense/T2 hyperintense on MRI. Lesions often infiltrative with capsular retraction. Lesions often non-enhancing or mildly enhancing; no diffusion restriction typical. Rapid increase in size possible.  
   Cons: None significant. The presence of a cutaneous myofibroma is supportive.  

4) Neuroblastoma:  
   Pros: Common solid tumor in infants and children. Can metastasize to liver.  
   Cons: Usually presents later than 2 days of age; liver mets uncommon at birth. Lesions usually show enhancement and diffusion restriction; no enhancement and no diffusion restriction argue against. Also no mass in adrenal or primary tumor mentioned.  

5) Haemangiomata:  
   Pros: Common benign liver lesion in infants; hypoechoic on US; T1 hypointense/T2 hyperintense.  
   Cons: Usually present earlier as lesions; tend to demonstrate characteristic enhancement (peripheral nodular, centripetal fill-in) on contrast imaging; no enhancement here argues against. Capsular retraction unusual; no diffusion restriction matches but enhancement is key.  

C) Ranking with tie-breaks:  
1) Infantile myofibromatosis (Iâ¢M) - best fits cutaneous lesion, infiltrative hepatic lesions with capsular retraction, rapid growth, no enhancement, no diffusion restriction.  
2) Lymphoma - less likely due to age and lack of diffusion restriction.  
3) Neuroblastoma - unlikely without adrenal primary or typical enhancement.  
4) Hepatoblastoma - enhancement typically present and slower growth.  
5) Haemangiomata - no enhancement or typical pattern and capsular retraction uncharacteristic.  

<final>Infantile myofibromatosis</final><|return|>
options: Hepatoblastoma | Lymphoma | Infantile myofibromatosis | Neuroblastoma | Haemangiomata

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 56-year-old woman with a known history of retroperitoneal sarcoma, who was receiving radiotherapy, was referred for evaluation of newly palpable lumps in her left breast and left axillary region.

 On the ultrasound of the left breast and axilla (Figures 2a and 2b), there are two adjacent hypo-echoic masses with circumscribed borders in the left breast at 2
 
 o’clock; m
 
 oreover, there is a similar mass in the left axillary region.
 
 The abdominal CT scan with contrast (Figures 3a and 3b) shows a large heterogeneously enhancing mass in the retroperitoneal space with left kidney invasion and aortic wall encasement.
 
 The histopathologic exam reveals highly atypical spindle cells with large pleomorphic hyperchromatic nuclei and high nucleo/cytoplasmic ratio (Figure 4).

Clinical History: A 19-year-old male presented to the orthopaedic surgeon with complaints of recurrent left shoulder pain. The patient practices combat sports and associates the complaints with a recent fall during training. A shoulder magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) was conducted, followed by MRI arthrography (MRA) due to inconsistent findings.
 Imaging Findings: MRI revealed oedematous changes in the inferior glenohumeral joint capsule, with increased signal intensity on the fat-saturated proton-density (PDFS) sequence, suggestive of an
 
 inferior glenohumeral ligament
 
 (
 
 IGHL) lesion (Figures 1a, 1b, and 1c).
 
 Additionally, there was an increased signal intensity in the superior labrum, which may indicate a labral lesion.
 
 However, this finding was considered non-specific due to the absence of intra-articular fluid or contrast (Figure 1a).
 
 Subsequent MRA revealed an intrasubstance full-thickness rupture of the anterior band of the IGHL with contrast extravasation (Figures 2a and 2b). This finding was not documented on the previous conventional MRI, which only indicated capsular oedema.
 
 Additionally, the axillary pouch changed from its normal U-shape to a J-shape on fat-saturated T2-weighted sequences (Figures 3a and 3b),
 
 indicating IGHL laxity and loss of its stabilising function
 
 .
 
 MRA also identified a
 
 superior labrum from anterior to posterior (
 
 SLAP) lesion (Figures 2c, 2d, and 3c), which was not clearly visible on MRI due to lack of fluid joint distention.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 67-year-old female patient diagnosed with a malignant renal mass underwent a CT examination for cancer staging. Her general symptoms were interpreted at that time within the oncological context.
 Imaging Findings: Contrast-enhanced CT examination of the thoracic, abdominal and pelvic regions was performed. Multiple images with tissular densities, mostly located peripherally, were identified in both lung areas. The described images ranged in size from a few millimetres (Figures 1a and 1b) to around 22x15 mm (Figures 2a and 2b), and enhanced during the arterial phase similarly to pulmonary vascular structures (Figures 3 and 4), with feeding vessels from lobar arteries and drainage into lobar veins (Figures 5a, 5b, 6a, and 6b).
PostDescription:
Clinical History: A 67-year-old female patient diagnosed with a malignant renal mass underwent a CT examination for cancer staging. Her general symptoms were interpreted at that time within the oncological context.
 Imaging Findings: Contrast-enhanced CT examination of the thoracic, abdominal and pelvic regions was performed. model_answer_raw:
<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>
A) Key findings:
- Patient: 67-year-old female with known malignant renal mass undergoing cancer staging CT.
- Imaging: Contrast-enhanced CT of thoracic, abdominal, and pelvic regions.
- Lung findings: Multiple tissular density images, mostly peripheral in both lung fields.
- Size range: few millimeters to about 22x15 mm.
- Enhancement pattern: enhances during arterial phase similarly to pulmonary vascular structures.
- Vascular supply: feeding vessels from lobar arteries and drainage via lobar veins.

B) Candidate pass/fail with pros/cons:
1. Hypervascular lung metastases
   - Pros: Occur in cancer patients; peripheral nodules; can be hypervascular.
   - Cons: Typically do not show feeding systemic arteries and venous drainage akin to vascular lesions; usually parenchymal lesions without distinct feeding/draining vessels; enhancement not exactly like vessels.
2. Pulmonary sequestration
   - Pros: Aberrant systemic arterial supply.
   - Cons: Usually located in lower lobes, often more isolated; lesions are non-functioning lung tissue with systemic arterial supply from aorta, not lobar pulmonary arteries; not multiple peripheral nodules.
3. Bronchocoele
   - Pros: Peripheral lung lesions.
   - Cons: Air-filled structures, not enhancing vascular lesions; not vascular on CT; no feeding/draining vessels.
4. Pulmonary varix
   - Pros: Vascular dilatation associated with pulmonary veins.
   - Cons: Usually single or few large venous dilatations; not multiple peripheral nodules; no feeding arterial vessels.
5. Pulmonary arterial aneurysm
   - Pros: Vascular lesion enhancing like arteries; arterial feeding.
   - Cons: Typically aneurysmal dilatations, often solitary; multiple nodules less common; no venous drainage like veins; usually no feeding veins from pulmonary arteries.
6. Pulmonary arteriovenous malformations (AVMs)
   - Pros: Typically peripheral; enhance during arterial phase; have feeding arteries and draining veins; multiple lesions possible; enhancing like vascular structures.
   - Cons: None significant; fits well.
7. Aberrant systemic to pulmonary communications
   - Pros: Abnormal connections.
   - Cons: Usually single or few; this typically refers to systemic artery to pulmonary vein connections; no mention of systemic arteries; findings show feeding from lobar arteries (pulmonary arteries) not systemic.

C) Ranking with tie-breakers:
1) Pulmonary arteriovenous malformations: Best fits peripheral location, arterial enhancement, feeding arteries from lobar arteries, and venous drainage; multiple lesions typical in some contexts.
2) Hypervascular lung metastases: Less typical feeding/draining vessels pattern; more parenchymal.
3) Rarely others fit less well.

<final> Pulmonary arteriovenous malformations </final><|return|>
options: Hypervascular lung metastases | Pulmonary sequestration | Bronchocoele | Pulmonary varix | Pulmonary arterial aneurysm | Pulmonary arteriovenous malformations | Aberrant systemic to pulmonary communications

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 39-year-old man presented to the emergency department with tibial and fibular fractures and a traumatic brain injury (TBI) after a motor vehicle accident.
 Imaging Findings: Imaging studies obtained after the trauma revealed a comminuted wedge fracture of the tibial diaphysis with mild displacement and angulation, along with an overlapping fibular fracture (Figure 1), and multifocal subarachnoid haemorrhage in the right frontoparietal and left parietal regions on brain CT (Figure 2).
 
 Six months after the initial injury, during which the patient remained bedridden without any new trauma history, shoulder X-rays and CT scans were performed due to complaints of shoulder pain (Figures 3a, 3b, and 3c). The imaging revealed extensive bilateral heterotopic ossification in the lower glenohumeral joint, indicating mature ossification.
PostDescription:

Six months after the initial injury, during which the patient remained bedridden without any new trauma history, shoulder X-rays and CT scans were performed due to complaints of shoulder pain. <|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>
A) Key findings:
- 39-year-old man with tibial and fibular fractures plus traumatic brain injury from motor vehicle accident.
- Six months post-injury, patient developed extensive bilateral heterotopic ossification in the lower glenohumeral joints.
- No new trauma during the interval and the patient remained bedridden.
- Imaging shows mature ossification.

B) Candidate pass/fail:
1) Calcific tendinitis
   - Pro: Can cause shoulder pain and calcifications.
   - Con: Typically involves calcifications within tendons, not extensive heterotopic mature bone formation in the joint; normally unilateral and not associated with trauma or brain injury. The described lesions are extensive and mature ossification, not calcific deposits.
   - Verdict: FAIL

2) Neurogenic heterotopic ossification
   - Pro: Classically occurs after CNS injury (TBI in this case), manifesting as heterotopic bone formation at periarticular sites such as the shoulder.
   - Con: None. This perfectly fits mature extensive bilateral heterotopic ossification in a paraplegic after TBI patient.
   - Verdict: PASS

3) Myositis ossificans
   - Pro: Involves heterotopic bone formation in muscles, often following trauma.
   - Con: Usually traumatic localized muscle injury; imaging often shows a “zone phenomenon” with maturation pattern. However, neurogenic heterotopic ossification is different and typically occurs after neural injury. Also, the lesions here are mature and bilateral, not recent or zonal.
   - Verdict: FAIL

4) Vascular, granulomatous or metabolic disease
   - Pro: Some metabolic diseases cause ectopic calcifications.
   - Con: No indication of such systemic disease in history; lesions are mature bone formation, not metabolic calcifications; bilateral extensive HO in the shoulder after TBI and immobilization is more suggestive of neurogenic etiology.
   - Verdict: FAIL

5) Fibrodysplasia ossificans progressiva
   - Pro: Genetic disease causing progressive heterotopic ossification.
   - Con: Typically presents in childhood, with progressive heterotopic bone formation at multiple sites; no mention of family history or early onset; the sudden onset after TBI and immobilization points more to neurogenic HO.
   - Verdict: FAIL

6) Tumoral calcinosis
    - Pro: Massive periarticular calcified masses.
    - Con: Usually associated with metabolic abnormalities (elevated phosphate), not mature bone; doesn't fit the traumatic neural injury context; no mention of metabolic disease.
    - Verdict: FAIL

7) Sarcoma
    - Pro: Can present as mass lesion.
    - Con: Imaging shows mature ossification, bilateral, typical for heterotopic ossification; sarcomas are usually unilateral and aggressive; no suspicion of malignancy.
    - Verdict: FAIL

C) Ranking:
1) Neurogenic heterotopic ossification (top, matches TBI & bedside care; typical site and matured lesions)
2) Myositis ossificans (close, but typical pattern and context less fitting)
3) Fibrodysplasia ossificans progressiva (genetic, earlier onset)
4) Tumoral calcinosis
5) Calcific tendinitis
6) Vascular, granulomatous or metabolic disease
7) Sarcoma

<final> Neurogenic heterotopic ossification </final><|return|>
options: Calcific tendinitis | Neurogenic heterotopic ossification | Myositis ossificans | Vascular | granulomatous or metabolic disease | Fibrodysplasia ossificans progressiva | Tumoral calcinosis | Sarcoma

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: 81-year-old woman who was presumed to be suffering from polymyalgia rheumatica and was receiving 2.5mg/week of ledertrexate. The patient suffered from chondrocalcinosis of the wrist joints, bilateral arthritis in her hands and repeated bouts of plurifocal synovitis in her wrists and metacarpophalangeal joints. The patient presented for an ultrasound due to Achilles tendon pain due to suspected bilateral Achilles tendonitis.
 Imaging Findings: Multiple hyperechogenic elongated deposits with acoustic shadowing are located within the central part of the right Achilles tendon and following its long axis (Figures 1 and 2), as well as in the left Achilles tendon (Figures 3 and 4). The Achilles tendons are thickened and hypoechogenic. The findings were identical in both Achilles tendons.

4) Hydroxyapatite deposits - less likely given typical sites and deposit morphology.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 45-year-old athlete with chest discomfort and palpitations presented to the cardiologist. Electrocardiogram showed low-density ventricular extrasystoles at rest, which increased in density with stress. Echocardiography revealed mild mitral regurgitation and mild left ventricular hypertrophy
 
 with a basal septal thickness of 12 mm.
 Imaging Findings: Cardiac magnetic resonance was performed on a 3 Tesla MRI scanner. Steady-state free precession (SSFP) sequences identify billowing of both mitral leaflets, with mitral valve prolapse (MVP) of the posterior leaflet (5 mm), mitral annular disjunction (6 mm) and “curling” (Figures 1a and 1b; yellow arrow). Mitral regurgitation was not significant. Biventricular function was normal.
 
 Late gadolinium enhancement (LGE) was present in the subendocardial–intramyocardial layer of the basal inferolateral wall, suggesting replacement fibrosis (Figures 2a, 2b, 2c, and 2d; white arrows). Native T1 mapping in the same region was elevated: 1360 ms (local normal range 1150–1250 ms), confirming myocardial fibrosis (Figures 2a, 2b, 2c, and 2d; yellow arrow). Cardiac CT ruled out significant stenosis in the circumflex artery and posterolateral artery (Figure 3; circumflex artery: yellow arrow).
PostDescription:

2) Malignant mitral valve prolapse with subendocardial late enhancement   (Less severe MR and no mention of high-risk arrhythmias but still consistent)  
3) Myocardial replacement fibrosis secondary to Ischemia in LCx territory   (Cardiac CT negative for stenosis, less likely)  
4) Not specific replacement fibrosis   (Too vague given clear MVP findings)  
5) Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy   (Mild LVH insufficient; pattern not typical)  
6) Replacement fibrosis related to sarcoidosis   (No supporting evidence)  
The best match is "Mitral valve prolapse related replacement fibrosis."

</analysis>

<final>Mitral valve prolapse related replacement fibrosis</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 63-year-old male, who had a renal transplantation from a cadaveric donor 3 months earlier due to diffuse mesangial proliferative glomerulonephritis.
 Imaging Findings: In view of worsening renal function indices and hyperpyrexia, the patient underwent an ultrasound which highlighted grade III hydro-nephrosis. The patient was admitted to our hospital to undergo a unilateral percutaneous nephrostomy
 
 procedure that was performed under fluoroscopy guidance and intravenous deep sedation. Because the graft was in the right iliac fossa
 
 , t
 
 he patient was supine.
 
 The antegrade pyelography revealed a stricture at the ureteral-bladder anastomosis and a
 
 tortuosity of the ureter
 
 , causing an occlusive narrowing of the ureter that prevented the contrast medium from passing into the bladder.
 
 Despite several attempts through multiple angles of the C-arm, from 18° cranial (CRA) to 34° caudal (CAU) and from 52° right (RAO) to 19° left (LAO) angulations, the ureteric bud cannot be visualised
 
 (Figures 1a, 1b, 1c, and 1d). After a failed attempt to overcome the ureteral narrowing, an 8.5 Fr nephrostomy drainage was placed in the renal pelvis.
 
 The second attempt was scheduled for 2 weeks later.
PostDescription:
Clinical History: A 63-year-old male, who had a renal transplantation from a cadaveric donor 3 months earlier due to diffuse mesangial proliferative glomerulonephritis.
 Imaging Findings: In view of worsening renal function indices and hyperpyrexia, the patient underwent an ultrasound which highlighted grade III hydronephrosis. The patient was admitted to our hospital to undergo a unilateral percutaneous nephrostomy procedure that was performed under fluoroscopy guidance and intravenous deep sedation. Because the graft was in the right iliac fossa, the patient was supine. The antegrade pyelography revealed a stricture at the ureteral-bladder anastomosis and a tortuosity of the ureter, causing an occlusive narrowing of the ureter that prevented the contrast medium from passing into the bladder. Despite several attempts through multiple angles of the C-arm, from 18° cranial (CRA) to 34° caudal (CAU) and from 52° right (RAO) to 19° left (LAO) angulations, the ureteric bud cannot be visualised. After a failed attempt to overcome the ureteral narrowing, an 8.5 Fr nephrostomy drainage was placed in the renal pelvis. The second attempt was scheduled for 2 weeks later.
DifferentialDiagnosisList: Post-transplant renal artery stent, Post-transplant renal vein stenting, Post-transplant ureteral stent removal, Post-transplant ureteral stent placement
model_answer_raw:
<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis> 
A) Key findings: 
- 63-year-old male with renal transplantation 3 months ago due to diffuse mesangial proliferative glomerulonephritis. 
- Worsening renal function indices and hyperpyrexia (fever). 
- Ultrasound showed grade III hydronephrosis. 
- Imaging was done under fluoroscopy guidance and sedation. 
- Antegrade pyelography revealed a stricture at the ureteral-bladder anastomosis and tortuosity of the ureter causing an occlusive narrowing that prevented contrast passage into the bladder. 
- Multiple C-arm angles attempted but the ureteric bud could not be visualized. 
- Successful placement of an 8.5 Fr nephrostomy drainage in the renal pelvis after failure to overcome the ureteral narrowing. 
- Second attempt scheduled for 2 weeks later.

B) Candidate pass/fail with pros/cons:
1) Post-transplant renal artery stent 
   - Pros: Could relate to transplant complications, and issues with arteries can cause renal failure.
   - Cons: Imaging findings describe ureteral/bladder anastomosis stricture, not arterial stenosis. No mention of arterial issues or findings consistent with artery problems. Also, hydronephrosis and pyelographic findings are not artery-related. 
   - Verdict: Not supported.

2) Post-transplant renal vein stenting 
   - Pros: Similar to artery, vein stents are used in transplant.
   - Cons: No mention of venous complications or venous stenting. Imaging findings are about ureter obstruction, not vein-related. Hydronephrosis and ureter obstruction can be caused by venous congestion but no evidence here. 
   - Verdict: Not supported.

3) Post-transplant ureteral stent removal 
   - Pros: Ureteral obstruction can occur after stent removal due to stricture formation or edema at anastomosis.
   - Cons: The imaging findings show a stricture and tortuosity at ureteral-bladder anastomosis causing occlusive narrowing, which could be consequent to recent stent removal. However, there is no explicit mention that a stent was previously placed and removed, but given typical management of transplant ureters involves temporary stenting, this is plausible.
   - Verdict: Strongly plausible.

4) Post-transplant ureteral stent placement 
   - Pros: Could be part of the attempt to relieve a ureteral obstruction.
   - Cons: The imaging described attempts to place a nephrostomy due to failure to overcome narrowing; no mention that a ureteral stent was successfully placed. Further, post-surgery or post-transplant initial stenting would usually be prophylactic; here we see a stricture at anastomosis causing obstruction; the text does not say a stent was placed. The current intervention was a nephrostomy placement to bypass obstruction. 
   - Verdict: Not strongly supported.

C) Ranking:
1) Post-transplant ureteral stent removal - Most consistent with anatomy and cause of stricture (post-op stricture after stent removal).
2) Post-transplant ureteral stent placement - Less likely because the case describes a stricture causing obstruction; intervention was nephrostomy placement.
3) Post-transplant renal vein stenting - No data supports venous involvement.
4) Post-transplant renal artery stent - No arterial findings described.

Thus, the best diagnosis is "Post-transplant ureteral stent removal" because the stricture at ureteral-bladder anastomosis causing obstruction and tortuosity is typical of issues occurring after removal of a ureteral stent given to maintain patency after transplantation.

</analysis><final>Post-transplant ureteral stent removal</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 24-year-old lady presented to the emergency department with accelerated hypertension. She had a history of recurrent episodes of sudden onset palpitations, headache and giddiness for the past two years, with uncontrolled hypertension despite being on multiple antihypertensive medications. Her blood investigations revealed elevated serum normetanephrine (1990 pg/mL).
 Imaging Findings: Trans-abdominal ultrasound (Figures 1a and 1b) showed a well-defined heterogeneously hyperechoic lesion in the right adnexa, seen separate from the right ovary, measuring approximately 4.7 x 5 x 4.8 cm, and with high internal vascularity.
 
 CT scan of the abdomen and pelvis (Figures 2a, 2b, 3a, and 3b) showed a hyperdense mass (average HU of 29) with intense heterogeneous arterial enhancement and few central non-enhancing areas suggestive of necrosis. It was located in the right adnexa and paracervical region, abutting the uterus (which was displaced to the left). It was abutting the posterior urinary bladder wall anteriorly and the rectum posteriorly with no invasion of the right ureter, iliac vessels and lateral pelvic side wall.
 
 On MRI of the pelvis, the lesion was deep to the lateral pelvic fascia on the right, heterogeneously iso to hyperintense on T1WI (Figures 4b and 5), heterogeneously hyperintense on T2WI (
 
 “light bulb sign”
 
 ) (Figure 4a), with areas of restricted diffusion on DWI and maintained fat planes with the adjacent structures (Figures 6a and 6b).
 
 On 68Ga-DOTATATE scan, there was intense homogeneous SSTR (somatostatin receptor) expression in a large well-defined soft tissue density mass in the right adnexa and paracervical region with maximum standardised uptake value (SUV max) of 20.19 (Figure 7).
