model_answer_raw:
<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>
A) Key findings:
- 24-year-old female with recurrent episodes of palpitations, headache, and giddiness.
- Accelerated hypertension unresponsive to multiple antihypertensives.
- Elevated serum normetanephrine (1990 pg/mL) indicating catecholamine excess.
- Imaging: Well-defined mass in the right adnexa/paracervical region measuring ~5 cm, separate from the right ovary.
- Ultrasound shows heterogeneously hyperechoic lesion with high internal vascularity.
- CT shows hyperdense mass (HU 29) with intense heterogeneous arterial enhancement and central necrosis.
- MRI: Heterogeneously iso to hyperintense on T1, heterogeneously hyperintense on T2 with "light bulb sign," restricted diffusion.
- The mass is deep to the lateral pelvic fascia and shows intense homogeneous somatostatin receptor expression on 68Ga-DOTATATE scan (SUV max 20.19).
- No invasion of adjacent critical structures (right ureter, vessels, pelvic side wall).

B) Candidate pass/fail:
- Solitary fibrous tumour: Usually arise from pleura but can be extrapleural; tend to be less vascular; no typical catecholamine secretion; no strong SSTR uptake; less likely in adnexa. (Fail)
- Pelvic paraganglioma: Catecholamine secretion consistent with elevated normetanephrine; intense hypervascularity on imaging; SSTR avid mass; location consistent with possible paraganglionic tissue in pelvis; typical "light bulb sign" on T2; fits clinical and imaging findings. (Pass)
- Hypervascular soft tissue sarcoma: Can be hypervascular and necrotic, but usually do not secrete catecholamines; no SSTR avidity; less likely. (Fail)
- Hypervascular lymphadenopathy (metastases or Castleman disease): Lymphadenopathy can be hypervascular, but Castleman disease might show intense enhancement but not typically catecholamine secretion; location somewhat possible but less likely with catecholamine excess. (Fail)
- Peripheral nerve sheath tumour: Can be hypervascular; location possible; but no catecholamine excess or SSTR avidity; inconsistent with elevated normetanephrine. (Fail)
- Aggressive angiomyxoma: Typically occurs in pelvis of young women but is usually myxoid with less intense enhancement; not catecholamine secreting; less vascular; no SSTR avidity. (Fail)

C) Ranking and final decision:
- Pelvic paraganglioma stands out as the most consistent with catecholamine excess (high normetanephrine), intense hypervascular enhancement with necrosis, "light bulb sign" on T2, intense SSTR expression on DOTATATE scan, and typical pelvic location separate from ovary. 
- All other diagnoses fail to explain catecholamine excess and SSTR avidity or are site inconsistent.

Therefore, the best diagnosis is "Pelvic paraganglioma."
<final>Pelvic paraganglioma</final><|return|>
options: Solitary fibrous tumour | Pelvic paraganglioma | Hypervascular soft tissue sarcoma | Hypervascular lymphadenopathy (metastases or Castleman disease) | Peripheral nerve sheath tumour | Aggressive angiomyxoma

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 63-year-old woman diagnosed with recurrent meningioma located in the foramen magnum consulted for headache and paraesthesia of the upper limbs. It was decided to perform decompressive surgery and, in the immediate postoperative period, she presented tetraplegia with preservation of consciousness and requirement of mechanical ventilation.
 Imaging Findings: The preoperative MRI showed an extra-axial expansive lesion located in the foramen magnum, measuring 3.9 x 2.1 cm, and producing a mass effect on the bulb and spinal cord, displacing them posteriorly and to the left. It also compromised the V4 segment of the right vertebral artery (Figures 1a, 1b, 1c, and 1d).
 
 The MRI on day +2 after the surgery showed signs of tumour persistence of the partially removed lesion (Figures 2a, 2b, and 2c) and signal alteration in the bulb and upper spinal cord, with hyperintensity in T2/FLAIR sequences (Figures 3a, 3b, and 3c) and hypointensity in T1. Diffusion sequences showed signs of mixed oedema (vasogenic-cytotoxic), all in relation to ischemic-oedematous phenomena (Figures 4a to 4i).

It also compromised the V4 segment of the right vertebral artery. The MRI on day +2 after the surgery showed signs of tumour persistence of the partially removed lesion and signal alteration in the bulb and upper spinal cord, with hyperintensity in T2/FLAIR sequences and hypointensity in T1. Diffusion sequences showed signs of mixed oedema (vasogenic-cytotoxic), all in relation to ischemic-oedematous phenomena.

model_answer_raw:
<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
- 63-year-old woman with recurrent meningioma at foramen magnum presenting with headache and upper limb paresthesia.  
- Underwent decompressive surgery; immediate postoperative tetraplegia but consciousness preserved, requiring ventilation.  
- Preoperative MRI: extra-axial lesion (3.9 x 2.1 cm) at foramen magnum causing mass effect on medulla oblongata ("bulb") and upper spinal cord, displacing them posteriorly and leftward; involvement of right vertebral artery V4 segment.  
- Postoperative MRI (day 2): residual tumour, altered bulb and upper spinal cord signal: T2/FLAIR hyperintensity, T1 hypointensity, diffusion showing mixed vasogenic-cytotoxic oedema consistent with ischemic-oedematous changes.  

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
1) Central cord syndrome  
   - Pros: Spinal cord injury involving central gray matter can cause motor weakness below the lesion level, often more pronounced in upper extremities.  
   - Cons: Usually due to trauma or hyperextension injuries, not typically postoperative ischemic injury; clinical presentation typically involves motor weakness with variable sensory loss, but no mention of tetraplegia with imaging signs of ischemia; also central cord syndrome usually spares the bulb and medulla; imaging does not usually show widespread cord hyperintensity on T2/FLAIR.  
2) White cord syndrome  
   - Pros: Postoperative phenomenon causing spinal cord injury and edema secondary to sudden decompression; characterized by T2 hyperintensity with ischemic changes; clinical tetraplegia after decompression; mixed vasogenic-cytotoxic edema; fits postoperative imaging and clinical presentation.  
   - Cons: Less commonly described in brainstem; however, bulb and upper spinal cord may be involved.  
3) Compressive myelopathy  
   - Pros: Preoperative mass effect from meningioma causing compression of spindle cord and bulb; clinically causing neurological deficits.  
   - Cons: However, postoperative MRI shows signal changes consistent with ischemia (diffusion restriction) and edema, indicating acute injury beyond chronic compression; compressive myelopathy alone does not explain sudden postoperative tetraplegia or the diffusion findings.  
4) Epidural haematoma  
   - Pros: Can cause acute neurological deficit postoperatively due to spinal cord compression.  
   - Cons: No described mass lesion consistent with haematoma on MRI; described lesion is tumour residue; no mention of hyperdense epidural collection or new mass; diffusion changes specific for ischemic edema rather than acute haematoma.

C) Ranking:  
1) White cord syndrome (exact postoperative ischemic-oedematous changes after decompression, matched clinical context)  
2) Central cord syndrome (does not fit precisely the surgical and imaging context)  
3) Compressive myelopathy (preoperative issue, but imaging shows postoperative ischemic changes)  
4) Epidural haematoma (no imaging evidence of haematoma)

<final> White cord syndrome </final><|return|>
options: Central cord syndrome | White cord syndrome | Compressive myelopathy | Epidural haematoma

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 29-year-old healthy woman presented with a 4-day history of right breast pain and periareolar bruising associated with a 1-day periareolar indurated tumefaction. She denied any other symptoms, trauma, or regular medication use. Physical examination revealed a 2-cm indurated, painful cord-like structure in the periareolar region associated with bruising and petechial lesions.
 Imaging Findings: Ultrasonography of the right breast revealed a subcutaneous, non-compressible, tubular hypoechoic structure with low-level internal echoes along an extension of approximately 28 mm (Figures 1 and 2). No blood flow was detected on colour or spectral Doppler studies (Figure 3). Furthermore, the adjacent soft tissues showed increased echogenicity. No other anomalies were seen in the right breast. Ultrasound scans of the left breast and axillary regions yielded no significant findings.

- Ultrasonography of the right breast shows a subcutaneous, non-compressible, tubular hypoechoic structure with low-level internal echoes extending about 2.8 cm, without blood flow on Doppler (suggesting thrombosis or fibrosis within a vessel or thrombosed vein).  
- Adjacent soft tissues have increased echogenicity (suggesting inflammation or edema).  
- No breast abscess, malignancy, or other lesions observed in both breasts.  
- No abnormal findings in left breast or axillae.  

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
- Mastitis: Usually presents with diffuse breast inflammation, redness, warmth, and possible cellulitis, often with tenderness and sometimes fever. It does not typically present with a focal cord-like structure or subcutaneous tubular hypoechoic lesion. PRO: Bruising and pain possible, but CONS: No ductal or lobular inflammation described; no cellulitis or diffuse edema typical for mastitis; and absence of systemic symptoms or risk factors (e.g., breastfeeding).  
- Breast abscess: Typically presents as a localized, poorly defined, hypoechoic or complex cystic mass with increased vascularity and sometimes gas formation; patient usually has systemic signs of infection. No abscess cavity or central necrosis described on ultrasound. PASS: Presence of a focal subcutaneous structure is inconsistent.  
- Inflammatory breast cancer: Presents with edema, skin thickening, reddened skin (peau d'orange), and a palpable mass or lump. The absence of a palpable mass and typical skin changes (peau d'orange) and the presence of a cystic cord-like subcutaneous lesion go against inflammatory breast cancer. DISQUALIFY.  
- Mammary duct ectasia: Presents with dilated ducts, periductal inflammation, and sometimes nipple discharge. No mention of ductal dilatation or nipple discharge. DISQUALIFY.  
- Subdermal cyst: Typically appears as an avascular, well-circumscribed cystic lesion, but not tubular or cord-like and lacks palpable pain and bruising. DISQUALIFY.  
- Mondor’s disease: Classic presentation includes a superficial, palpable, cord-like fibrotic thrombosis of the subcutaneous veins in the breast, most often in the periareolar region, with pain and possible skin changes (bluish discoloration, bruising). Ultrasound shows a tubular, hypoechoic, non-compressible structure with no blood flow. This matches the findings exactly. PASS.  
- Vasculitis: Typically presents with systemic signs and multiple vascular areas involved; could cause skin changes but rarely a single cord-like subcutaneous induration. Also would expect more widespread findings. DISQUALIFY.  
- Thrombophilic disorders: Can predispose to thrombosis, but usually present with systemic signs or thrombosis of deeper or multiple vessels, not a localized superficial breast cord-like lesion. DISQUALIFY.

Other diagnoses lower in ranking or excluded.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 53-year-old male patient presented with a lump in his left jaw, with noted enlargement in 2 months. There were no associated symptoms or relevant clinical history. Physical examination disclosed an expansile lesion in the left mandibular region and excluded palpable lymph nodes.
 Imaging Findings: Non-enhanced computed tomography (CT) dental scan showed an expansile lytic lesion centred to the angle and ascending ramus of the left mandible, measuring 4 cm at its largest axis, with well-defined, multilobulated borders and ground-glass ossified matrix (Figures 1a and 1b). The final report included osteosarcoma and chondrosarcoma as diagnostic hypotheses and the patient was referred to our tertiary hospital.
 
 Contrast-enhanced cervical CT performed at our hospital better depicted that the lesion did not invade adjacent structures (Figure 2) and enabled evaluation of the infra-hyoid neck. A solid, hypodense, 3.5 cm mass was identified posterior to the right thyroid lobe (Figure 3) and a provisional diagnosis of parathyroid adenoma was proposed.
 
 On ultrasound, the referred nodule was well-defined and hypoechoic (Figures 4a and 4b).
 
 Laboratory findings revealed elevated parathyroid hormone (PTH) (301pg/mL) and elevated calcium levels (13mg/dL).
 
 Parathyroid scintigraphy with technetium-99m methoxy-isobutyl-isonitrile (
 
 Tc-99m MIBI)
 
 was performed and an adenoma of the right inferior parathyroid was confirmed (Figures 5a and 5b).

- Scintigraphy: Right inferior parathyroid adenoma confirmed.
- The lesion is an expansile lytic mandibular lesion with ground-glass matrix.

  Pros: Expansile lytic mandibular lesion is typical location; age fits some cases.
  Cons: Giant cell tumours usually show radiolucent lesions without ground-glass matrix; lack of aggressive features such as cortical breach or soft tissue mass; no clinical data supporting typical giant cell tumor behavior; no mention of epiphyseal involvement (mandibular metaphysis involvement is typical but the matrix described here (ground-glass) is unusual); no systemic signs.

  Pros: Occurs in the mandible; expansile lytic lesions with some sclerosis possible.
  Cons: Usually occurs in younger patients, histologically characterized by fibroblast and giant cell proliferation with hemorrhagic/necrotic areas; radiological features typically less uniform; no mention of ground-glass matrix; more prevalent in children/young adults.

  Pros: Ground-glass matrix typical for FD; well-defined multilobulated borders; location (jaw) classic for craniofacial fibrous dysplasia; age fits; expansile lesion without soft tissue invasion.
  Cons: FD usually appears with a "ground-glass" matrix (described here); however, FD does not usually present with parathyroid adenoma or elevated calcium/PTH; absence of associated symptoms is typical.

  Pros: Secondary lesion to hyperparathyroidism (elevated calcium and PTH, parathyroid adenoma); classic site includes mandible; radiographically an expansile, lytic lesion can show ground-glass matrix or sclerosis; clinical and laboratory findings support parathyroid adenoma causing secondary bone lesions.

  Pros: Expansile lesion, can have ground-glass matrix; occurs in mandible; well-circumscribed.

2) Fibrous dysplasia: Ground-glass matrix and mandible location fit perfectly; however, no hyperparathyroidism or elevated calcium/PTH; brown tumour is more appropriate here given the endocrine findings.
3) Giant cell tumour: Possible but less likely because of ground-glass appearance and endocrine findings.
4) Ossifying fibroma: less likely due to endocrine findings and typical age.
5) Giant cell reparative granuloma: least likely because of age, radiologic features, and lack of associated symptoms.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 58-year-old male with worsening nausea for the past 72 hours presented to the emergency department with severe chest pain radiating to the back in the context of 4 recent episodes of non-bloody, non-bilious emesis. He was afebrile, diaphoretic, and mildly tachycardic. Cardiac workup, complete blood count, and electrolytes were normal.
 Imaging Findings: A chest X-ray followed by contrast-enhanced chest CT were performed. The X-ray demonstrated pneumomediastinum extending into the neck and a tiny left apical pneumothorax. Notably, left pleural effusion with atelectasis and consolidation were also seen (Figure 1a). The coronal CT images were closely correlated to the X-ray findings, clearly showing the pneumomediastinum extending superiorly towards the neck soft tissues. A pneumothorax at the left lower lung base, not previously seen on X-ray, is also demonstrated on coronal CT view (Figure 1b).
 
 The axial chest CT image on lung window showed evidence of pneumomediastinum with air around the oesophagus. Left pneumothorax and bilateral pleural effusions are also seen (Figure 2). On the soft tissue window, a thickened distal oesophagus was seen; however, no clear site of perforation was appreciated. Bilateral complex attenuating pleural effusions were also observed but there was no obvious contrast extravasation (Figure 3).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: Case 1 is a 54-year-old female who experienced right ankle pain after an injury, leading to the clinical suspicion of Achilles tendon rupture, and was evaluated by radiograph and MRI after 1 week.
 
 Case 2 is a 62-year-old male who reported calf pain, raising concerns for deep vein thrombosis, further evaluated with ultrasound. He noted a trivial trauma
 
 occurred 10 days prior.
 Imaging Findings: In Case 1, the lateral ankle radiograph revealed increased soft tissue thickness around the Achilles tendon with small calcification (Figure 1). MRI indicated significant disruption (95%) of the distal 5.5 cm of the tendon, cystic areas, and signs of haemorrhage (Figures 2a, 2b, 3a, 3b, and 4). Extensive oedema in the posterior lower leg muscles led to a screening of the leg that identified a 2.8 x 1.6 x 5.4 cm collection in the intermuscular fascial plane, causing indentation on the soleus. The plantaris tendon was partially torn, while the soleus and medial gastrocnemius exhibited grade 1 strain without fibre discontinuity (Figures 5a and 5b).
 
 In Case 2, the ultrasound showed a bulky distal 6.0 cm Achilles tendon with altered echotexture, multiple calcific foci, and a high-grade partial thickness tear (Figures 6a, 6b, and 6c). An anechoic collection measuring 3.3 x 1.2 x 8.6 cm was observed in the intermuscular fascial plane, between the gastrocnemius and soleus muscles, with no increased vascularity noted on the Doppler study (Figures 7a and 7b).

   - Pros: Both cases show significant disruption of Achilles tendon, especially distal portion in both.
   - Cons: Does not explain the presence of large collections in intermuscular fascial plane (fluid collections/hematoma) or muscle edema.

   - Pros: Tennis leg traditionally involves calf muscle rupture, often medial head of gastrocnemius. Case 2 has calf pain and ultrasound shows tendon tear and collection, which could fit.
   - Cons: Both cases also have significant Achilles tendon rupture, which is beyond a typical gastrocnemius strain. This diagnosis alone does not explain tendon disruption.

   - Pros: Accounts for both major findings: Achilles tendon rupture and calf muscle involvement with fluid collection. The intermuscular collections and muscle edema on MRI and US are consistent with tennis leg (medial gastrocnemius or soleus muscle strain/rupture). The presence of a bulky tendon with high-grade partial tear (case 2) also fits.
   - Cons: Rare but described in literature; matches the combination of findings perfectly.

4) Ruptured Baker’s cyst  
   - Pros: Can cause calf fluid collections and calf pain.
   - Cons: No mention of cystic mass or popliteal fossa involvement. The collections are intermuscular not cystic in popliteal fossa. Also, Achilles tendon rupture not explained.

5) Deep vein thrombosis  
   - Pros: Case 2 presented with calf pain; ultrasound was performed.
   - Cons: No vascular abnormalities noted; no echogenic filling defect in veins; no Doppler abnormalities noted; findings of tendon rupture and intermuscular collection do not fit DVT.

C) Ranking:  
1) Achilles rupture with tennis leg – best fits all findings: tendon rupture + intermuscular collections indicative of muscle injury/hematoma (tennis leg).  
2) Achilles rupture – explains tendon findings but not calf collections/muscle edema.  
3) Tennis leg – explains calf findings but not tendon rupture.  
4) Ruptured Baker's cyst – partial explanation of calf collection, but no cyst or tendon rupture.  
5) Deep vein thrombosis – ruled out by imaging and clinical findings.

Therefore, the best diagnosis is "Achilles rupture with tennis leg."
</analysis>  
<final>Achilles rupture with tennis leg</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 55-year-old male, with skin melanoma (IIIc), on adjuvant immunotherapy with nivolumab since 6 months,
 
 previously without comorbidities,
 
 presented with >5-fold increase in serum lipase and
 
 an onset of diabetes
 
 . Nivolumab was suspended till lipase decreased. After the reintroduction of nivolumab, another elevation of lipase
 
 and abdominal pain
 
 were
 
 noted.
 Imaging Findings: Computed tomography showed diffuse pancreatic parenchymal enlargement, heterogeneity (Figure 1), and slightly increased peripancreatic fat attenuation. There were no radiological signs of cholelithiasis or cholecystitis. Additionally, diffuse colon and rectal wall thickening and hyperenhancement were reported (Figure 2), as well as a triangular enhancement defect in the left kidney (Figure 3).
 
 At the second onset of symptoms, MRI was performed, demonstrating pancreatic volume increase, with signal alterations on T2-weighted images, especially in the pancreatic head, and diffuse restricted diffusion (Figures 4a, 4b, 5, 6a, and 6b). Additionally, mildly expressed peripancreatic and mesenteric fat stranding was noted. No pancreatic duct or biliary tree dilatation was observed. There were no fluid collections, free abdominal fluid or adenopathy.

<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
- 55-year-old male with stage IIIc melanoma on nivolumab immunotherapy.  
- Clinical presentation with repeated elevations in serum lipase (>5-fold increase) and new-onset diabetes.  
- Imaging: Diffuse pancreatic enlargement, heterogeneous attenuation, mild peripancreatic fat stranding, no biliary obstruction or gallstones.  
- Additional findings: diffuse colon and rectal wall thickening and hyperenhancement, left renal triangular enhancement defect.  
- MRI findings: increased pancreatic volume, T2 signal alterations especially in the head, diffuse restricted diffusion on DWI, mild fat stranding, no duct or biliary dilatation, no collections or lymphadenopathy.  
- Symptoms recurred after reintroduction of nivolumab, suggesting a causal link between the immunotherapy and pancreatic inflammation.  

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
- Viral pancreatitis: Typically presents with an acute onset of pancreatitis with pancreatic inflammation, but no mention of viral infection or typical viral prodrome; also, the relation to nivolumab and recurrence after reintroduction suggests an immune-mediated process rather than viral. PASS: 1 (viral pancreatitis can cause pancreatic inflammation), FAIL: 2 (lack of viral prodrome, typical imaging less specific).  
- Autoimmune pancreatitis (AIP): Classic features include diffuse pancreatic enlargement, heterogeneity, delayed enhancement, capsule-like rim, and restriction on MRI, which correlate well here. Presence of other autoimmune-like features like bowel wall thickening and kidney involvement also fit. However, patient is on immunotherapy, which could confuse idiopathic AIP with drug-induced AIP. But imaging findings and clinical context favour AIP. PASS: 1, PASS: 2.  
- Idiopathic pancreatitis: No idiopathic trigger here; the clear temporal relationship with nivolumab and recurrence on rechallenge argues against idiopathic. FAIL: 1.  
- Immune-related pancreatitis: The patient is on an immune checkpoint inhibitor (nivolumab), with clear temporal association of symptoms with therapy initiation and rechallenge causing relapse; imaging is consistent with pancreatitis. PASS: 1, PASS: 2 (imaging fits).  
- Chronic pancreatitis: Usually presents with calcifications, ductal changes, atrophy; here we have acute inflammatory changes with pancreatic volume increase and no ductal dilatation or chronic calcifications. FAIL.  
- Normal response to treatment with immune checkpoint inhibitors: The imaging findings show significant inflammation, lipase elevation, and clinical symptoms, not a benign or normal reaction to treatment. FAIL.  

C) Ranking with tie-breaks:  
1. Immune-related pancreatitis: Strong temporal relationship with nivolumab reintroduction and elevated lipase, diffuse pancreatic inflammation without signs of alcohol, gallstones or infection, and additional extra-pancreatic autoimmune features (bowel, kidney).  
2. Autoimmune pancreatitis: Imaging findings consistent, but the immune checkpoint blockade is a more direct cause. AIP is also often treated with steroids and can be associated with extra-pancreatic findings like bowel changes. However, the therapy (nivolumab) and timing make immune-related pancreatitis more likely.  
3. Viral pancreatitis: Possible but less likely; no viral infection noted.  
4. Idiopathic pancreatitis: Less likely due to clear drug relationship and extra-pancreatic findings.  
5. Chronic pancreatitis and normal response are unlikely based on the clinical and imaging data.  

Given the clinical context and imaging findings, the diagnosis of immune-related pancreatitis fits best.

</analysis>  
<final>  
Immune-related pancreatitis  
</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: An 81-year-old male patient on anticoagulation therapy, with a history of hip fracture and acute renal insufficiency, underwent a standard chest X-ray as part of the preoperative evaluation for a total hip replacement. He was asymptomatic on the respiratory level, with no dyspnoea or chest pain.
 Imaging Findings: The initial X-ray revealed a likely pleural right peripheral opacity with smooth borders and no acute angles (Figure 1).
 
 A CT scan was then performed, demonstrating an extra-pleural collection measuring 30 × 72 × 87 mm (AP × TR × CC). The collection was spontaneously hyperdense, suggesting the presence of blood or fluid with a high protein content. Additionally, the “extra-pleural fat sign” was observed, confirming an extra-pleural process rather than a pleural or lung lesion (Figures 2 and 3).
 
 In the presence of renal insufficiency and an extra-pleural mass, contrast injection was not performed. Consequently, a mass with potential bleeding could not be definitively ruled out, and an MRI was conducted. T1-weighted MRI images showed the extra-pleural collection appearing hyperintense due to the presence of blood products (Figure 4). In T2-weighted images, the lesion was mildly hyperintense, consistent with the presence of subacute haemorrhage or an organised haematoma (Figure 5).
 
 On diffusion-weighted imaging (DWI), a hyperintense signal with ADC restriction was observed (Figures 6a and 6b). After gadolinium injection, a peripheral enhancement of the lesion’s capsule was observed, with no enhancement observed within the lesion itself (Figure 7).
 
 This supports the primary haemorrhagic nature of the lesion rather than an inflammatory or neoplastic process.
 
 Non-contrast CT and MRI scans of the thorax were performed using T1, T2, DWI, and T1 fat-saturated gadolinium sequences in a Philips Ingenia 1.5T high-field MRI scanner and a Philips Incisive 32-slice CT scanner.

model_answer_raw:
<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
- An 81-year-old male on anticoagulation therapy with a history of hip fracture and acute renal insufficiency.  
- Asymptomatic respiratory status with no dyspnea or chest pain.  
- Initial chest X-ray showed a pleural right peripheral opacity with smooth borders and no acute angles, suggesting an extra-pleural location.  
- CT confirmed an extra-pleural collection measuring 30x72x87 mm, spontaneously hyperdense, implying blood or high-protein fluid content.  
- The "extra-pleural fat sign" confirmed its extra-pleural location.  
- MRI T1 hyperintensity consistent with blood products.  
- T2 mildly hyperintense suggesting subacute hemorrhage or organized hematoma.  
- DWI hyperintensity with ADC restriction (indicates restricted diffusion).  
- Gadolinium-enhanced MRI showed peripheral capsule enhancement with no internal enhancement, supporting a non-neoplastic, hemorrhagic lesion.  
- Contrast CT not performed due to renal insufficiency, limiting exclusion of bleeding mass.  

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
1) Encysted pleural effusion  
   - Pro: Pleural/peripheral location, fluid collection.  
   - Con: Typically not hyperdense; no capsule enhancement; usually no restricted diffusion or T1 hyperintensity due to blood products. Encysted effusions are usually fluid-filled, not hemorrhagic.  
2) Empyema  
   - Pro: Can be encapsulated; located in pleura.  
   - Con: Usually shows internal enhancement or septations; not spontaneously hyperdense unless infected with high protein content; would show inflammatory features; patient asymptomatic; no signs of infection.  
3) Pleural malignant neoplasm  
   - Pro: Extra-pleural mass with enhancement possible.  
   - Con: No internal enhancement; peripheral capsule only; T1 hyperintensity (blood products) not typical; no signs of malignancy such as solid components or irregular margin; also capsule enhancement with no internal uptake suggests inflammation or organization, not neoplasm.  
4) Extra-pleural haematoma  
   - Pro: Hyperdense on CT (blood), T1 hyperintensity on MRI (blood products), DWI restriction consistent with clot or organized hematoma, peripheral capsular enhancement only, no internal enhancement, extra-pleural location confirmed by "extra-pleural fat sign", risk factor of anticoagulation.  
   - Con: None significant; best fits all imaging and clinical findings.  
5) Parapneumonic effusion  
   - Pro: Pleural fluid collection possible.  
   - Con: No infection signs; no internal enhancement; T1 hyperintensity unlikely; patient asymptomatic; hyperdensity and enhancement pattern not typical.  
6) Solitary fibrous tumour  
   - Pro: Could be extra-pleural mass.  
   - Con: Would likely show internal enhancement; usually not hyperdense on non-contrast CT; no blood products on MRI; capsule enhancement only with no internal mass not typical for a tumor.  
7) Intercostal nerve tumour  
   - Pro: Could present as extra-pleural mass.  
   - Con: Unlikely spontaneously hyperdense, no blood products; typical enhancement pattern different; no neural symptoms; not fitting imaging signs.  

C) Ranking:  
1) Extra-pleural haematoma (best fit: imaging features of hyperdensity, T1 hyperintensity, restricted diffusion, peripheral capsule enhancement only, extra-pleural location, anticoagulation risk factors).  
2) Encysted pleural effusion (second, but lacks hemorrhagic features).  
3) Empyema (infection signs and internal enhancement absent).  
4) Pleural malignant neoplasm (lack of internal enhancement and blood signal argues against).  
5) Solitary fibrous tumour (rarely hemorrhagic, typically internal enhancement).  
6) Parasymptotic effusion (infection signs absent, unlikely).  
7) Intercostal nerve tumour (least likely by imaging and symptoms).  

<final>Extra-pleural haematoma</final><|return|>

Clinical History: A 64-year-old man with a prior history of pulmonary tuberculosis presents with an acute onset of significant haemoptysis. He mentions experiencing weight loss, tiredness, and intermittent fever. His medical history is unremarkable, except for tuberculosis, which was treated a decade ago. A chest X-ray performed recently demonstrated cavitary abnormalities.
 Imaging Findings: CT angiography provided the main findings, consisting of a localised aneurysm of a pulmonary artery branch located near a tuberculous cavity (Figures 2a and 2b), representing the aneurysm. Hyperdense opacity with surrounding haematoma indicates active bleeding, while multiple air locules within the opacity are characteristic of a tuberculous cavity (Figures 1a and 1b), distinguishing it from other pulmonary lesions. Centrilobular ground-glass nodules may be observed around the haematoma, suggesting endobronchial dissemination of blood (Figure 4). Post-contrast imaging reveals focal enhancement of the aneurysm, aiding in precise assessment of its size, shape, and location (Figures 3a, 3b, and 3c).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 2-year-old female was admitted to the emergency department due to a 2-week history of constipation, drooling, and left eye strabismus due to oculomotor nerve paralysis. Further physical examination without clinically significant findings. Initial laboratory tests upon admission showed white blood count (WBC), neutrophils, and lymphocytes within normal ranges.
 Imaging Findings: An initial contrast-enhanced CT scan of the brain (Figures 1a, 1b, 1c, and 1d) showed a lesion that extended to cranial structures and temporal lobes, involving deep neck spaces.
 
 An initial contrast-enhanced thoracoabdominal CT scan (Figures 2a, 2b, 2c, and 2d) showed pleural thickening, subpleural nodules, pancreatic lesions with dilated pancreatic duct calibre, bilateral renal cortical lesions, and a right paraspinal lesion invading the spinal canal at L2–L3 with nerve roots involvement, suggestive of metastasis. Pelvic lymphadenopathies were identified.
 
 A 2-month follow-up contrast-enhanced MRI (Figures 3a, 3b, 3c, and 3d) revealed a smaller lesion compared to the previous CT, with decreased intracranial and cerebral extension and a reduced cerebral and cerebellar volume.
 
 The 2-month follow-up contrast-enhanced thoracoabdominal CT scan (Figures 4a, 4b, 4c, and 4d) showed no pleural thickening areas or subpleural nodules. The pancreas with a size reduction of the focal lesions. The pancreatic duct calibre was preserved, and the kidneys had no infiltrative lesions. Generalised reduction of pelvic lymphadenopathy. The paraspinal lesion was not visible. A not previously identified generalised fatty infiltration was shown.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: In an ambulatory appointment, a 29-year-old female patient presented with chronic and persistent cough. No smoking history was referred, and the physical examination was unremarkable. An ambulatory chest x-ray was performed in our institution and further evaluation with computed tomography (CT), followed by magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), was later requested.
 Imaging Findings: The chest x-ray (Figures 1a and 1b) revealed an opacity, with rounded and well-defined borders, localised in the posterior and medial regions of the right middle lung field.
 
 Contrast-enhanced thoracic CT /Figures 2a, 2b, 2c, and 2d) revealed in the right paravertebral region, next to the posteromedial aspect of the 7
 
 th
 
 and 8
 
 costal arches, a large nodular lesion, with 47 x 49 x 41 mm (L x AP x T), ovoid with regular and well-defined borders, heterogeneous and with marked peripheral contrast uptake. It is localised in an extrapulmonary topography
 
 , as it is centred on the chest wall and shows obtuse angles with the thoracic wall, and it is near proximity to
 
 nerve structures. There were no signs of invasion of adjacent structures, particularly bone structures.
 
 The contrast-enhanced thoracic MRI images (Figures 3a, 3b, 3c, 3d, and 3e) confirmed the localisation and nature of the mass, further characterising it as a soft tissue lesion showing heterogeneity of signal in the T2-weighted sequences, with areas of interstitial hyperintensity and markedly heterogeneous uptake after contrast administration.

It is localised in an extrapulmonary topography, as it is centred on the chest wall and shows obtuse angles with the thoracic wall, and it is near proximity to nerve structures. There were no signs of invasion of adjacent structures, particularly bone structures. A) Key findings:

- CT: Large nodular lesion in right paravertebral region near 7th and 8th costal arches; ovoid, well-defined, heterogeneous; marked peripheral contrast uptake; extrapulmonary (centered on chest wall) with obtuse angles to thoracic wall; no bone invasion; near nerve structures.  
- MRI: Soft tissue lesion; T2 heterogeneity with interstitial hyperintensity; markedly heterogeneous contrast enhancement.  

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
1) Intercostal nerve schwannoma (neurilemoma)  
   - Pros: Occurs near nerve structures; well-defined, homogeneous or heterogeneous enhancement; can be extrapulmonary and paravertebral; typical size and morphology match.  
   - Cons: Usually shows nerve sheath tumor characteristics such as target sign on MRI (central hypointensity, peripheral hyperintensity), which wasn't explicitly described; heterogenous peripheral enhancement fits but not exclusive.

2) Neurofibroma  
   - Pros: Soft tissue nerve sheath tumor; can be paravertebral and extrapulmonary; heterogeneous on T2 due to myxoid and fibrous components; peripheral enhancement possible.  
   - Cons: Typically less well-circumscribed; often associated with neurofibromatosis (not mentioned); tend to be more infiltrative, which was not seen here.

3) Ganglioneuroma  
   - Pros: Benign nerve sheath tumor; can be posterior mediastinum/paravertebral; large, well-defined mass; heterogeneous T2 hyperintensity due to myxoid stroma.  
   - Cons: Usually occurs in younger patients, can be in mediastinum but often more central; heterogenous contrast uptake possible but marked peripheral uptake is less typical; the location is chest wall extrapulmonary rather than mediastinum.

4) Paraganglioma  
   - Pros: Highly vascular tumor; marked and often intense peripheral contrast enhancement; can be near nerve structures.  
   - Cons: Usually in paraganglionic sites (e.g., aorticopulmonary region, carotid body), rarely paravertebral chest wall; often shows "salt and pepper" appearance on MRI with flow voids, not mentioned; no bone invasion but typical sites are different.

C) Ranking:  
1) Intercostal nerve schwannoma (neurilemoma) - best fits location (paravertebral chest wall, nerve structures), morphology (well-defined, heterogeneous peripheral enhancement), and age group.  
2) Neurofibroma - similar nerve sheath origin, but less well circumscribed and no neurofibromatosis noted.  
3) Ganglioneuroma - plausible but less typical location and enhancement pattern.  
4) Paraganglioma - high vascularity fits enhancement, but location and typical MRI appearance less likely.

options: Intercostal nerve schwannoma (neurilemoma) | Neurofibroma | Ganglioneuroma | Paraganglioma

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 46-year-old male patient presented to our department for routine follow-up imaging after left radical orchiectomy 19 years earlier due to a testicular germ cell tumour, and with retroperitoneal recurrence 11 years after surgery. He had a previous history of left orchidopexy during childhood to repair cryptorchidism.
 Imaging Findings: In this patient, who underwent a left radical orchiectomy for a testicular germ cell tumour, the abdominal-pelvic computed tomography (CT) revealed tubular-shaped splenic tissue along the left paracolic gutter, representing a retroperitoneal splenic cord connecting the spleen to the remaining mesonephric structure in the left lower quadrant (Figures 1a to 1i). Additionally, this condition was associated with congenital absence of lower limbs (Figure 2).
 
 Retrospectively, the described abdominal features were considered stable compared to the oldest examination available in our database, which was performed at the time of the retroperitoneal recurrence (Figures 3a to 3f).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 3-year-old female with poor feeding toleration, aspirating all consistencies of food, with failure to thrive, was planned for gastrostomy tube placement, and an upper gastrointestinal study was requested for the anatomic assessment.
 Imaging Findings: A fluoroscopic upper gastrointestinal study was done by injection of barium via the indwelling enteric tube. The stomach exhibited a normal appearance and was normally emptied through a normal pylorus, duodenal bulb, and descending duodenum (Figure 1). After an initial descent of the duodenum, the third part of the duodenum did not cross the midline as expected. Instead, it ascends on the right side of the spine posterior and parallel to the descending duodenum up to the level of the duodenal bulb and then completes its transverse course and reaches the left upper abdomen (Figures 2 and 3).

- The third part of the duodenum did not cross the midline as expected. Instead, it ascended on the right side of the spine, posterior and parallel to the descending duodenum, up to the level of the duodenal bulb, then crossed to the left upper abdomen to complete its transverse course.  

B) Candidate pass/fail with pros/cons:  
1) Duodenum inversum:  
   Pros: This condition is characterized by a reversal of the normal course of the third part of the duodenum, which ascends on the right side and then crosses, consistent with findings described. The imaging findings exactly describe the third part not crossing the midline as usual, but moving upward and then crossing, which aligns with duodenum inversum.  
   Cons: Duodenum inversum is rare and often associated with other anomalies, but no other mention here. However, the imaging description fits well with its known description.  

2) Redundant duodenum:  
   Pros: Redundant duodenum can cause abnormal loops and serpentine courses of the duodenum. Could theoretically present with abnormal duodenal courses.  
   Cons: Redundant duodenum usually involves excess redundant loops or elongations, not a reversal or elevation of the third part. The description here does not mention excess loops, kinking, or redundancy of the duodenum, only abnormal position of the third part. Also, normal pylorus and descending duodenum are described.  

3) Intestinal malrotation:  
   Pros: Malrotation can cause abnormal position of the duodenum and abnormal crossing of the midline.  
   Cons: Usually malrotation is characterized by abnormal position of the duodenojejunal junction, which is normally to the left of the spine; also, there may be a misplaced cecum. The specific pattern of the third part ascending and then crossing is characteristic for duodenum inversum rather than non_rotation or malrotation.  

C) Ranking:  
1) Duodenum inversum: best matches the unique description of the third part ascending on the right side before crossing the midline, reversing the normal anatomy.  
2) Intestinal malrotation: potential consideration due to abnormal duodenal positioning but less specific and less typical imaging features.  
3) Redundant duodenum: less likely; no description of redundant loops or elongation, only abnormal positioning.

Clinical History: A 50-year-old female patient presented with headache, visual disturbances, and bitemporal hemianopia. Hormonal assays indicated abnormal levels of serum prolactin and growth hormone.
 Imaging Findings: The patient’s MRI brain showed a large lobulated sellar lesion (Figure 1) on post-contrast sagittal images, showing suprasellar extension and anteriorly extending into both sphenoid sinuses and ethmoid sinuses. Axial T2 image (Figure 2) shows the lesion to be predominantly involving the left cavernous sinus and encasing the left internal carotid artery, and seen extending into the left orbital apex and left frontal and temporal lobes. FLAIR coronal image (Figure 3) shows compression of adjacent brain parenchyma, lateral ventricle, and brainstem. In the post-contrast axial image (Figure 4), the lesion was seen extending into the right cavernous sinus with suprasellar extension into both frontal lobes and sphenoid sinuses.

A) Key findings:
- Patient: 50-year-old female with headache, visual disturbances, and bitemporal hemianopia.
- Hormonal assays: abnormal serum prolactin and growth hormone levels.
- Imaging: Large lobulated sellar lesion on MRI post-contrast.
- Lesion characteristics: Suprasellar extension, anterior extension into both sphenoid and ethmoid sinuses.
- Involvement: Predominantly left cavernous sinus, encasing left internal carotid artery, extending to left orbital apex and left frontal and temporal lobes.
- Additional extension: extension into right cavernous sinus, suprasellar extension into both frontal lobes and sphenoid sinuses.
- FLAIR coronal image: compression of adjacent brain parenchyma, lateral ventricle, and brainstem.

B) Candidate pass/fail:

1. Meningioma:
   - Pros: Meningiomas can occur in parasellar regions, invade cavernous sinus, encase internal carotid artery, and extend into adjacent bone sinuses. They can have extensive lobulated appearance and cause mass effect.
   - Cons: The lesion is primarily in the sellar region with suprasellar and cavernous sinus involvement, which is less typical for meningioma (though possible). Hormonal abnormalities are not a typical feature associated with meningioma. Bitemporal hemianopia could be due to optic chiasm compression, which could be seen with meningiomas, but hormone abnormalities argue against.

2. Pituitary macroadenoma:
   - Pros: Common cause of sellar mass, usually presents with headache, visual disturbances, bitemporal hemianopia due to chiasm compression. Hormonal abnormalities (prolactin and growth hormone) fit exactly with functional pituitary macroadenoma. Suprasellar extension and cavernous sinus invasion are known aggressive features of macroadenomas. Bilateral cavernous sinus involvement and invasion into sphenoid/ethmoid sinuses are aggressive but can be seen in macroadenomas.
   - Cons: The extensive bone sinus invasion might be more unusual, but aggressive macroadenomas can invade bone and sinuses. Encasing ICA might be more common with invasive tumors like macroadenoma.

3. Chordoma:
   - Pros: Chordomas arise from notochord remnants, usually midline clivus or sacrum; can involve clivus and extend to cavernous sinus and sinuses rarely. They cause midline skull base masses.
   - Cons: The lesion is described in the sellar region, not clivus/midline skull base. Hormonal abnormalities (prolactin, GH) are incompatible with chordoma. Clinical symptoms (visual disturbances, bitemporal hemianopia) point to pituitary involvement, not clival compression. Axial T2 imaging findings and extensive lobulated lesion are not typical for chordoma. Chordoma does not secrete hormones.

C) Ranking with tie-breaks:

- 1st: Pituitary macroadenoma (fits hormonal profile, sellar location, suprasellar extension, cavernous sinus invasion)
- 2nd: Meningioma (fits some aspects of extension and cavernous sinus involvement, but hormonal abnormalities argue against)
- 3rd: Chordoma (doesn't fit location, hormonal profile, and typical imaging)

Pituitary macroadenoma

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: Five days after an uncomplicated embolisation of an asymptomatic, growing right middle cerebral artery (MCA) aneurysm, a 56-year-old woman comes to the ER due to headache, a seizure and fluctuant left upper extremity weakness and left campimetric defects. A Todd’s paralysis is suspected, and the patient is admitted for a workup.
 Imaging Findings: Initially, a brain CT is performed, which shows no significant abnormalities apart from the embolisation material (Figure 1).
 
 Then a brain MRI is performed, including the following sequences: DWI (Figures 2a and 2b), SWI (Figure 3), T2, FLAIR (Figure 4), 3D TOF and pre and post-gadolinium T1 (Figures 5a and 5b). The MRI discloses multiple round lesions varying in size in the right hemisphere. The lesions are spontaneously T1 hypointense and T2 hyperintense, the largest ones showing a small, central T2 hypointensity. Post-gadolinium enhancement is demonstrated in all the lesions. No restricted diffusion or susceptibility artefacts are present.
 
 Follow-up imaging after 6 months (Figures 6, 7, 8a, and 8b) shows overall improvement with resolution of the enhancing foci. However, T2 hyperintensity is still present, and central punctate susceptibility artefacts have appeared, interpreted as microhaemorrhagic foci.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 75-year-old female presented with a four-day history of sudden onset frontal headache, vomiting, ophthalmalgia, photophobia and blurred vision. Her past medical history included migraines, right-sided phacoemulsion and an intraocular lens implant six years ago. Fundoscopy demonstrated choroidal folds and effusions. Laboratory blood workup revealed only elevated serum ACE levels.
 Imaging Findings: Initial computerised tomography (CT) head imaging and magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) of the head were unremarkable. Specifically, CT did not identify any orbital pathology. MRI of the orbits revealed bilateral effusions in the suprachoroidal space, with low signal intensity on T1 sequences (Figure 1) and high-intensity signal on T2 sequences (Figure 2). Findings were more pronounced on the right side. Effusions extended anteriorly past the ora serrata. Contrast-enhanced sequences with fat suppression visualised choroidal and scleral enhancement (Figure 3). There was no evidence of intraocular mass or retinal detachment.
 
 A subsequent high-resolution CT chest scan revealed biapical scarring, mild bronchial wall thickening and subtle fissure nodularity in the upper zones (Figure 4) and a 10 mm subcarinal lymph node (Figure 5). Thoracic imaging findings were non-specific and equivocal for sarcoidosis.

1) Systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE)  
- Pros: SLE can cause ocular inflammation and serous retinal detachments.  
- Cons: No systemic symptoms or laboratory markers supporting SLE mentioned; presence of elevated ACE is not typical; imaging findings not classical for SLE-related ocular disease; choroidal effusions are less common in SLE; no mention of other systemic features.  
- Verdict: Fail.  

2) Choroidal detachment and posterior scleritis  
- Pros: Bilateral suprachoroidal effusions; choroidal and scleral enhancement on MRI are classic signs of posterior scleritis; pain (ophthalmalgia) and photophobia fit clinical picture.  
- Cons: Posterior scleritis usually presents with acute painful red eye; systemic sarcoid involvement possible but not mandatory.  
- Verdict: Pass.  

3) Acute glaucoma  
- Pros: Patient reports blurred vision and photophobia; glaucoma can cause headache.  
- Cons: Fundoscopy did not indicate optic disc swelling or typical glaucomatous changes; imaging does not show elevated intraocular pressure related findings; no mention of optic nerve sheath dilation. Absence of typical glaucomatous findings. Effusions not characteristic.  
- Verdict: Fail.  

4) Sarcoidosis  
- Pros: Elevated serum ACE; chest CT findings (though nonspecific) with upper-zone nodularity and lymphadenopathy support sarcoidosis; ocular sarcoid involvement can cause choroidal folds and effusions; typical choroidal and scleral enhancement is seen in inflammatory sarcoid ocular disease.  
- Cons: Chest CT findings are equivocal; no biopsy confirmation; however, findings are suggestive and not conflicting.  
- Verdict: Pass.  

5) IgG4 related disease  
- Pros: Can cause orbital inflammation and inflammatory changes.  
- Cons: No mention of IgG4 levels, typical imaging findings (e.g., lacrimal gland enlargement), or characteristic involvement of other tissues; less common ocular manifestations.  
- Verdict: Fail.  

6) Vogt-Koyanagi-Harada (VKH) syndrome  
- Pros: Bilateral serous retinal detachments (suprachoroidal effusions), choroidal and scleral enhancement consistent; ocular inflammation and effusions typical; usually presents with photophobia and headache.  
- Cons: Age and sex less typical (young adults more common), but possible. Elevated serum ACE can be present due to granulomatous inflammation, but not specific. No extraocular symptoms (e.g., neurological, cutaneous) mentioned.  
- Verdict: Pass but less likely due to age and lack of systemic signs.

1) Choroidal detachment and posterior scleritis

2) Sarcoidosis  
- Elevated serum ACE, chest imaging suggestive but nonspecific. Ocular inflammation fits, but no definitive systemic confirmation.  
3) Vogt-Koyanagi-Harada (VKH) syndrome  
- Fewer systemic features reported, patient older than typical for VKH, but ocular findings compatible.  
4) Systemic lupus erythematosus  
5) IgG4 related disease  
6) Acute glaucoma  

Final decision: The most likely diagnosis based on imaging and clinical data is choroidal detachment with posterior scleritis. It fully explains the suprachoroidal effusions, choroidal/scleral enhancement, and ocular symptoms without requiring additional systemic disease for confirmation. Elevated ACE and subtle chest findings can be explained by sarcoid granulomatous inflammation but are not essential for diagnosis here.

</analysis>  
<final>  
Choroidal detachment and posterior scleritis  
</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 13-year-old boy presented with pain over the left hip for the last 3 years and a gradually enlarging mass for the last 2 years. Upon physical examination, the left hip range of motion was reduced. Lab tests showed normal calcium, high serum phosphorous of 7.07 Eq/dL, and normal parathormone levels.
 Imaging Findings: A radiograph of the left hip demonstrated multilobulated periarticular calcifications (Figure 1). MRI (Figures 3a, 3b, 3c, 3d, and 3e) showed a well-defined, lobulated lesion affecting the glutei, vastus lateralis, and obturator externus muscles. Multiple fluid-calcium levels, termed the “sedimentation sign”, were noted, with calcifications appearing hypointense on both T1 and T2-weighted sequences, and fluid appearing hypointense on T1-weighted and hyperintense on T2-weighted sequences. These lesions exhibited heterogeneous contrast enhancement. CT imaging revealed a multilobulated calcific mass without bone or joint involvement (Figures 2a and 2b).
 
 Histopathological examination (Figures 4a and 4b) confirmed the diagnosis of tumoral calcinosis, revealing fibro-collagenous tissue with granular calcifications arranged in lobules with surrounding multinucleated giant cells, foamy macrophages, lymphocytes, and plasma cells.

These lesions exhibited heterogeneous contrast enhancement. CT imaging revealed a multilobulated calcific mass without bone or joint involvement. model_answer_raw:
<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
- Patient: 13-year-old boy with 3-year history of left hip pain and 2-year history of gradually enlarging mass.  
- Physical exam: reduced left hip range of motion.  
- Lab tests: normal calcium, elevated serum phosphorus (7.07 Eq/dL), normal parathormone.  
- Radiograph: multilobulated periarticular calcifications around left hip.  
- MRI: well-defined, lobulated lesion in gluteus and adjacent muscles (vastus lateralis and obturator externus). Multiple fluid-calcium levels ("sedimentation sign") observed; calcifications hypointense on T1/T2; fluid hypointense on T1 and hyperintense on T2; heterogeneous contrast enhancement.  
- CT: multilobulated calcific mass, no bone or joint involvement.  
- Histopathology: fibro-collagenous tissue with granular calcifications in lobules, surrounded by multinucleated giant cells, foamy macrophages, lymphocytes, plasma cells.  
- Overall: multilobulated, periarticular soft tissue calcifications with sedimentation sign, elevated phosphate, normal calcium and PTH, no bone involvement, typical histology.  

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
1) Soft tissue sarcoma  
   - Pros: mass with heterogeneous enhancement, soft tissue lesion.  
   - Cons: no bone/joint involvement is nonspecific, but sarcomas usually do not have multilobulated calcifications with sedimentation sign; no mention of malignancy histologically. Unlikely given the histopathology showing benign fibro-collagenous and inflammatory changes.  
2) Myositis ossificans  
   - Pros: periarticular ossific/calcific lesion, soft tissue involvement.  
   - Cons: usually involves mature bone formation with zonal ossification pattern, often after trauma; here no bone formation noted, no trauma history, and fluid-calcium levels with sedimentation sign are not typical.  
3) CPPD disease (Calcium pyrophosphate deposition disease)  
   - Pros: periarticular calcifications.  
   - Cons: typically articular cartilage and joint involvement; here lesion is mainly soft tissue muscles; patient age (13 years) is unusual; no mention of chondrocalcinosis.  
4) Hydroxyapatite deposition disease  
   - Pros: calcifications in soft tissues.  
   - Cons: usually smaller, peripheral deposits causing acute pain; no multilobulated mass formation or sedimentation sign; patient is younger than typical.  
5) Hyperparathyroidism  
   - Pros: abnormal calcium-phosphorus metabolism.  
   - Cons: calcium is normal, PTH normal, no bone lesions like brown tumors; not consistent.  
6) Primary hyperphosphatemic tumoral calcinosis  
   - Pros: classic multilobulated periarticular calcifications, sedimentation sign fluid-calcium levels; elevated phosphate, normal calcium and PTH; typical histology; lesion in gluteal muscles and periarticular soft tissues, no bone involvement.  
   - Cons: none significant, fits perfectly.  
7) Synovial osteochondromatosis  
   - Pros: periarticular joint involvement with calcified bodies.  
   - Cons: usually intra-articular calcifications, loose bodies; here mass is soft tissue, multilobulated, no bone/joint involvement described; no cartilage nodules described; age and clinical presentation atypical.  

C) Ranking:  
1) Primary hyperphosphatemic tumoral calcinosis – pathognomonic findings: multilobulated soft tissue calcifications, sedimentation sign, elevated phosphate, normal calcium/PTH, characteristic histology.  
2) Myositis ossificans – possible but lacking typical zonal ossification and trauma history.  
3) Soft tissue sarcoma – unlikely given histology and imaging features.  
4) Synovial osteochondromatosis – typically intra-articular.  
5) CPPD disease – no typical chondrocalcinosis or joint cartilage involvement.  
6) Hydroxyapatite deposition disease – pattern and size inconsistent.  
7) Hyperparathyroidism – labs and imaging inconsistent.  

<final> Primary hyperphosphatemic tumoral calcinosis </final>
<|return|>
options: Soft tissue sarcoma | Myositis ossificans | CPPD disease | Hydroxyapatite deposition disease | Hyperparathyroidism | Primary hyperphosphatemic tumoral calcinosis | Synovial osteochrondromatosis

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 73-year-old woman presented with abdominal pain, mild jaundice and dark urine for 3 days. Initial blood tests revealed anaemia and elevation of serum creatinine, total bilirubin, and lactate dehydrogenase. Further analytical evaluation excluded autoimmune and microangiopathic haemolytic anaemias. Flow cytometry analysis of peripheral blood showed a deficiency of GPI-anchored proteins. Abdominal ultrasound did not reveal any significant findings, so an abdominal magnetic resonance (MRI) was subsequently conducted.
 Imaging Findings: MRI excluded acute biliary pathology but revealed abnormal bilateral renal cortical hypointensity on both T2 (Figure 1) and T1-weighted (Figure 2) sequences. In gradient-recalled echo (GRE) imaging, the renal cortex demonstrated a relative increase in signal intensity on the out-of-phase sequence (Figure 3) compared to the in-phase sequence (Figure 2). Reduced cortical enhancement was observed after intravenous contrast administration (Figures 4a, 4b, and 4c). The spleen and liver showed no significant parenchymal signal abnormalities (Figure 5). Isolated renal haemosiderosis was presumed.
 
 Abdominal ultrasound and computed tomography did not reveal significant abnormalities.
PostDescription:

 Imaging Findings: MRI excluded acute biliary pathology but revealed abnormal bilateral renal cortical hypointensity on both T2 and T1-weighted sequences. In gradient-recalled echo (GRE) imaging, the renal cortex demonstrated a relative increase in signal intensity on the out-of-phase sequence compared to the in-phase sequence. Reduced cortical enhancement was observed after intravenous contrast administration. The spleen and liver showed no significant parenchymal signal abnormalities. DifferentialDiagnosisList: Transfusion-related haemosiderosis, Mechanical valvular haemolysis, Paroxysmal nocturnal haemoglobinuria, Chronic haemolytic anaemia, Haemochromatosis

<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>
A) Key findings:
- 73-year-old woman with abdominal pain, mild jaundice, dark urine for 3 days.
- Lab: anemia, elevated serum creatinine, total bilirubin, lactate dehydrogenase.
- Flow cytometry: deficiency of GPI-anchored proteins.
- Imaging: MRI shows abnormal bilateral renal cortical hypointensity on T1 and T2; GRE imaging shows relative increase in signal intensity on out-of-phase sequence compared to in-phase sequence; reduced cortical enhancement after contrast.
- No parenchymal signal abnormalities in liver and spleen. Ultrasound and CT unremarkable.

B) Candidate pass/fail with pros/cons:

- Transfusion-related haemosiderosis:
  - Pro: bilateral renal cortical hypointensity could suggest iron overload.
  - Con: Usually occurs after multiple transfusions; patient presentation and lab findings do not mention transfusions. The GRE out-of-phase signal increase suggests intracellular iron rather than blood products. Also, haemosiderosis typically causes hypointensity on GRE sequences due to magnetic susceptibility from iron.
  - Lab findings of GPI-anchor deficiency point away from transfusion-related cause.

- Mechanical valvular haemolysis:
  - Pro: hemolysis could cause anemia and elevated bilirubin.
  - Con: Flow cytometry finding of GPI-anchored protein deficiency does not fit mechanical hemolysis. Typically, mechanical hemolysis does not cause RBC surface protein deficiency. Renal changes not characteristic.
  
- Paroxysmal nocturnal haemoglobinuria (PNH):
  - Pro: laboratory finding of deficiency of GPI-anchored proteins is pathognomonic for PNH. Clinical features of hemolysis, anemia, elevated bilirubin, and elevated LDH are consistent. MRI findings of renal cortical hypointensity and GRE signal changes are consistent with iron deposition due to renal hemosiderin from hemoglobinuria. Reduced cortical enhancement supports renal involvement.
  - Con: Liver and spleen normal, which aligns with PNH (no extravascular hemolysis typically in spleen/liver).

- Chronic haemolytic anaemia:
  - Pro: similar lab findings possible.
  - Con: No GPI-deficiency. Renal iron deposition less specific. Clinical course less consistent with chronicity.

- Haemochromatosis:
  - Pro: iron deposition causing hypointensity on MRI.
  - Con: Usually liver, pancreas, heart involved; spleen often affected. No liver or spleen abnormalities here. Also, no mention of typical iron overload signs or liver signal changes. GPI deficiency does not fit.

C) Ranking:
1) Paroxysmal nocturnal haemoglobinuria (PNH) - flow cytometry shows GPI-deficiency, renal MRI findings consistent with hemosiderin deposition from hemoglobinuria.
2) Chronic haemolytic anaemia - similar symptoms but lacks GPI-deficiency; renal findings less typical.
3) Haemochromatosis - possible T1/T2 hypointensity from iron, but no liver/spleen involvement, less likely.
4) Transfusion-related haemosiderosis - less likely given no transfusions mentioned and GPI-deficiency.
5) Mechanical valvular haemolysis - unlikely given specific lab findings and renal imaging.

<final>Paroxysmal nocturnal haemoglobinuria</final><|return|>
options: Transfusion-related haemosiderosis | Mechanical valvular haemolysis | Paroxysmal nocturnal haemoglobinuria | Chronic haemolytic anaemia | Haemochromatosis

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 45-year-old male with a history of glioblastoma, submitted to surgical resection followed by Stupp protocol 11 years ago, and organic psychosis, with therapeutic noncompliance and multiple hospitalisations in recent years, presented to the emergency department with acute onset gait disturbance, right-sided muscle weakness, numbness and behavioural changes.
 Imaging Findings: A head CT performed upon admission revealed a status post left parietal craniotomy, with a cortico-subcortical temporoparietal surgical site exhibiting gliosis, stable when compared to previous imaging. No new hypodensities suggestive of stroke were observed, and CT angiography excluded arterial occlusion.
 
 Subsequent brain MRI T1-weighted post-contrast images demonstrated
 
 de novo
 
 cortical gyriform enhancement in the previously treated surgical site, specifically in the temporoparietal region, with extension into the occipital, insular and remaining temporal lobe. Perfusion arterial spin labelling (ASL) study indicated increased cerebral blood flow (CBF) in the affected area. Importantly, there were no signs of acute stroke nor tumour progression, as evidenced by the absence of restricted diffusion, an extension of the high T2 FLAIR signal or nodular enhancement.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: 26-year-old female with no relevant past medical history presented with a two-year history of worsening pain at the right proximal humerus and associated weakness.
 Imaging Findings: X-ray of the right shoulder (Figures 1a and 1b) showed an expansile lucent proximal humeral meta-diaphysis lesion with well-defined medial and craniocaudal margins, narrow zone of transition, mild lateral endosteal scalloping and subtle medial periosteal reaction.
 
 CT of the right shoulder (Figures 2a and 2b) showed a predominantly fat-density soft tissue mass and an extensive area of full-thickness cortical loss anteriorly with focal permeation of the posterior cortex of the meta-diaphysis.
 
 Subsequent MRI (Figures 3, 4a, 4b, 4c, and 4d) confirmed a 4.8 cm maximum diameter predominantly fat-signal lesion within the proximal humerus with mildly enhancing internal curvilinear soft tissue stranding and more nodular soft tissue superiorly with speckled intra-lesional fat and extensive full thickness cortical loss and medial cortical permeation with secondary stress fracture. Mildly enhancing mild peri-lesional marrow and cortical oedema were also noted. There was no extra-osseous invasion.
 
 Nuclear medicine whole-body scan revealed avid uptake at the right proximal humerus with no other disease.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 3-month-old girl was brought to casualty with a history of intermittent fever for 1 month, abdominal distension, and straining while passing urine and stools for 4 days. On examination, a soft, tender, non-mobile midline mass was palpated in the suprapubic region.
 Imaging Findings: Transabdominal greyscale ultrasound revealed a large, well-defined cystic lesion in the pelvis with dense mobile echoes (Figure 1). The mass was seen posterior to the bladder and anterior to the rectum and was extending into the abdominal cavity up to the umbilicus. The cystic structure was in continuity with the stretched uterine cavity (Figure 2). Suprapubic probe tenderness was positive. There was no evidence of any solid component or calcifications within. Both the ovaries were seen separately from this lesion. Bilateral kidneys showed mild hydronephrosis. Bowel loops were normal in course, calibre, and peristalsis and were displaced to the periphery. On transperineal ultrasound examination, there was a thin membrane of 1 mm thickness at the vaginal orifice (Figure 3).

   - Cons: Fever present could support infection; however, no mention of pus or infection signs in ultrasound; also, no solid components or pus pockets described. Thin membrane at vaginal orifice could allow drainage, but pyometrocolpos usually associated with complete obstruction.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A middle-aged patient presented with what were initially suspected to be new-onset migraines but then developed auditory and visual hallucinations and complex paranoid beliefs. They became confused and intermittently incoherent with deteriorating coordination, becoming unsteady and sustaining several falls. The clinical picture was further complicated by the onset of myoclonus and a rapidly progressive cognitive decline, which raised suspicion for a neurodegenerative condition.
 Imaging Findings: The patient initially underwent a CT which showed only generalised cerebral atrophy.
 
 They then had further investigations with an MRI, including diffusion-weighted imaging (DWI) and corresponding apparent diffusion coefficient (ADC) images. This revealed widespread restricted diffusion involving almost the entire cortex except the superior temporal gyrus and also affecting the caudate bilaterally and the left lentiform nuclei. Fluid attenuated inversion recovery (FLAIR) imaging showed a more subtle high signal in these areas. Other than some small and non-specific foci of high signal in the deep white matter, there were no further positive findings.

1) Hypoxic encephalopathy:  
   - Pro: Diffuse cortical restricted diffusion can be seen in hypoxic injury.  
   -  Con: Typically affects watershed zones and basal ganglia symmetrically; patient has no history of acute hypoxia event; clinical picture more neurodegenerative.

   - Pro: Can cause diffuse cortical changes and neuropsychiatric symptoms.  
   - Con: Restricted diffusion is less common; usually affects the insular cortices and cingulate gyri; no mention of liver disease signs or labs; clinical picture not classic for metabolic encephalopathy.

   - Pro: Can cause diffuse cortical restricted diffusion.  
   - Con: Typically involves basal ganglia and hippocampi; clinical context often includes an acute hypoglycaemic episode, which is not mentioned; here the distribution is widespread cortical excluding superior temporal gyrus, less typical.

   - Con: None; findings are classic.

   - Pro: Can cause cortical FLAIR hyperintensity and restricted diffusion; neuropsychiatric symptoms possible.  
   - Con: Usually focal or lobar, often temporal lobes predominance; no mention of inflammatory signs or clinical infection; widespread cortical diffusion restriction less typical.  

C) Ranking with tie-breaks:  
1) Creutzfeldt–Jakob disease  
2) Hypoxic encephalopathy  
3) Encephalitis  
4) Hypoglycaemic encephalopathy  
5) Hyperammonaemia (Hepatic encephalopathy)  

The hallmark findings of cortical ribboning with restricted diffusion sparing the superior temporal gyri, basal ganglia involvement, rapid clinical decline with myoclonus, and neuropsychiatric symptoms strongly point to Creutzfeldt–Jakob disease as the most likely diagnosis.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 41-year-old woman presented with worsening perineal pain and pustular skin lesions. Examination revealed extensive erythematous and pustular lesions in the perineal and perianal regions, with rope-like dermal thickening and induration. Multiple fistulous openings with purulent and haemoserous discharge were noted.
 Imaging Findings: Ultrasound of the perineal and perianal regions revealed dermal and hypodermal band-like structures indicative of fistulas/tunnels, with multiple dermal collections around hair follicles containing hyperechoic hair fragments (Figures 1a, 1b, and 1c). Some hypodermal fluid collections were connected to the epidermis via sinus tracts (Figure 1d). Power Doppler showed increased vascularity, suggesting active inflammation (Figure 1e).
 
 MRI demonstrated skin thickening at the perineum and labia majora, hypertrophic scarring, and deep subcutaneous fistulas/tunnels extending into the ischioanal space and perianal region (Figures 2a and 2b), with left adductor muscle involvement suggestive of myositis (Figure 2c). The tunnels exhibited post-contrast rim enhancement, indicating active inflammation, but no fistulas communicating with the anal canal lumen were identified.
 
 After immunomodulatory therapy, significant lesion reduction was observed, with resolution of the fistulas and myositis. Residual skin thickening and scarring were noted, with minimal post-gadolinium enhancement, indicating minor active inflammation (Figures 3a and 3b).

   - Pros: Classic presentation with sinus tracts, fistulas, induration, multiple pustular lesions, hair fragment inclusions in collections, typical perianal/perineal location; typical "rope-like" dermal thickening; immunomodulatory therapy response consistent with Hidradenitis suppurativa treatment.

3) Perianal fistula - non-specific and requires underlying cause which best fits hidradenitis.  
4) Fournier gangrene - unlikely due to chronicity and lack of acute necrotizing features.  
5) Carbuncles - incompatible with chronic sinuses and fistulas.

   - Cons: Fournier gangrene is a rapidly progressive necrotizing fasciitis, would show air in tissues, systemic toxicity, more acute presentation; no mention of necrotizing, gas, or systemic signs typical of Fournier gangrene; chronicity and response to immunotherapy argue against this.  

5) Carbuncles  
   - Pros: Skin lesions with pus.  
   - Cons: Carbuncles are localized acute infections with a central necrosis and are usually not chronic, with no fistulous tracts or deep subcutaneous involvement; rope-like thickening and chronic sinus tracts are not typical.  

C) Ranking:

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 52-year-old male presented to the outpatient clinic with swelling over the right greater trochanteric region, which progressively grew after a fall four months before. He had a previous history of neoadjuvant radiotherapy followed by resection of a soft tissue sarcoma in the left thigh seven years before.
 Imaging Findings: On the preoperative MRI performed seven years before (Figures 1, 2a, 2b, and 2c), an intramuscular lobulated mass was seen in the left tensor fascia lata, extending into the subcutis. The mass showed areas of high signal on T1WI (Figure 2a), which became hypo-intense after fat suppression (Figure 2b). The mass was heterogeneous, predominantly hyperintense on T2WI (Figure 2b) and showed internal areas of heterogeneous enhancement after gadolinium contrast (Figure 2c).
 
 The current MR study revealed a thick-walled, well-defined lesion superficial to the right tensor fascia lata, with heterogeneous high signal on T1WI (Figures 3a and 3b) and T2WI (Figures 4a and 4b), persisting after fat suppression. Multiple internal fluid-fluid levels were present. While post-contrast T1WI sequences (Figure 5) showed intralesional heterogeneous high signal, the dynamic subtraction sequence (Figure 6), only showed a thick peripheral enhancing rim. Therefore, the pseudoenhancement seen on the static sequences was due to a haemorrhage-related high native T1WI signal.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 63-year-old female, with a history of anterior cervical diskectomy and fusion, had a CT head and cervical spine in 2007 for unrelated symptoms. Incidentally, imaging identified a heavily calcified lesion in the right cerebellar hemisphere. The patient remained asymptomatic and, therefore, opted for regular follow-up imaging as opposed to surgical management.
 Imaging Findings: The CT brain demonstrated a 2.5 cm (maximal diameter), highly calcified intra-axial lesion with no surrounding oedema or mass effect, within the right cerebellar fossa (Figures 1 and 2).
 
 The MRI demonstrated the same lesion within the right cerebellar hemisphere, measuring 2.5 cm (maximal axial measurement). The lesion abutted the inferior tentorial surface; however, a broad base against it was not exhibited. There was no notable mass effect or surrounding oedema. On T1 (Figure 3) and T2 (Figure 4) weighted images, the lesion displayed a low signal intensity. Additionally, blooming artefacts were observed on gradient imaging (Figure 5).
 
 During the 17-year follow-up period, the patient exhibited no symptoms. Biennially, MRI heads revealed no change in the lesion size or associated mass effect.

<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
- Patient: 63-year-old female, asymptomatic over 17 years with stable lesion on imaging.  
- Lesion: 2.5 cm heavily calcified intra-axial lesion in the right cerebellar hemisphere.  
- Imaging: CT shows high calcium density, no perilesional edema, no mass effect. MRI shows low T1 and T2 signal (consistent with calcium), blooming artifact on GRE (indicating susceptibility, consistent with calcification or blood products).  
- Location: Right cerebellar hemisphere, abutting but not broadly basing on the inferior tentorial surface (suggests not dural-based).  
- Clinical course: Stable size and no symptoms over prolonged follow-up.  

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
1) Calcified meningioma  
   - Pros: Calcification is common in meningiomas, and they can be intra-axial or extra-axial; slow growth and stability are possible.  
   - Cons: Typically dural-based with a broad base, which this lesion lacks; mostly extra-axial location; the cerebellar hemisphere is less common for meningiomas; no dural tail described.  

2) Calcified cavernoma  
   - Pros: Cavernomas can calcify and have low T1/T2 signal; location plausible.  
   - Cons: Usually associated with hemorrhagic components with hemosiderin or mixed signal on T1/T2; blooming artifact can be present but typical cavernoma appearance includes a "popcorn" heterogeneous signal due to blood products, not purely low signal calcification; may cause some surrounding changes.  

3) Calcified pseudoneoplasm of the neuraxis (CAPNON)  
   - Pros: CAPNONs are known for heavy calcification, low T1/T2 signal, blooming artifact on GRE, and are usually well-circumscribed benign lesions; intracranial CAPNONs are rare but documented in parenchymal locations including cerebellum; usually stable and asymptomatic.  
   - Cons: Rare lesion, but fitting hallmark imaging findings; no perilesional edema or mass effect which aligns with benign nature.  

4) Oligodendrogliomas  
   - Pros: Frequently calcified intracranial tumors; located in the cortex or juxtacortical white matter but can occur in parenchyma.  
   - Cons: Usually intra-axial but more supratentorial than infratentorial; typically not heavily calcified or purely low signal on T1/T2; can cause some mass effect or edema; in older adults, less common in cerebellum; no malignant transformation signs here.  

5) Choroid plexus papilloma  
   - Pros: Can calcify, typically intraventricular or attached to choroid plexus surfaces.  
   - Cons: Typically extra-axial, intraventricular; this lesion is intra-axial in cerebellar hemisphere, no mention of CSF spaces or ependymal surfaces; less likely.  

C) Ranking:  
1) Calcified pseudoneoplasm of the neuraxis (CAPNON)  
2) Calcified cavernoma  
3) Calcified meningioma  
4) Oligodendroglioma  
5) Choroid plexus papilloma  

- CAPNON best fits stable, heavily calcified intracerebellar lesion with low signal and blooming artifact, no edema or mass effect, and no dural attachment.  
- Cavernoma less likely due to absence of typical blood product signal heterogeneity.  
- Meningioma unlikely due to lack of dural base.  

</analysis>  
<final>Calcified pseudoneoplasm of the neuraxis</final><|return|>
